segments.s668-g177: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a past-time clause. The following sequence is subject + object + verb: بهرام (subject) بهرام را (definite object marked by را) دید is the simple past 'saw'. گفت is the past 'said' and functions as the speech verb that governs the following content clause.
segments.s668-g178: که introduces the reported content ('that/...'). هرگز is the negator 'never'. مبادی is used here as 'origins/beginnings'. تو 'you' is the addressee. با is the preposition 'with'. خاک 'earth/dust'. جفت functions as 'paired/matched (with)' — together the clause says your origins will never be matched/paired with the earth.
segments.s668-g179: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / from then on' (از + آن + پس). بپرسیدش is the past/stative/interrogative form 'he asked him' (بپرسید- + -ش object clitic). از before ترک here marks the topic 'about/concerning' and ترک is 'Turk' used as an ethnic noun; زشت is an epithet 'ugly/vile' modifying ترک.
segments.s668-g180: که‌ای is the fused form of the interrogative/addresses (که + ای) meaning 'O who...' introducing a vocative question. دوزخی is an adjective 'hellish' or a noun 'damned one' modifying روی 'face' (روی = 'face'). دور از بهشت is a prepositional phrase 'far from paradise', where دور 'distant' combines with از + بهشت 'from paradise'. The whole is an apostrophic address: 'O hell-faced one, far from paradise...'.
segments.s668-g181: چه introduces an interrogative 'what/which'. مردی here is a noun 'man' or 'manliness' asking about status; و simply coordinates. نام نژاد تو چیست is a standard interrogative sequence 'and what is the name of your lineage?' — تو is the 2nd-person pronoun. چی ... ست functions as the copular question 'what is ...?' (چی = what, ست = is). Word order is interrogative: question word + predicate + copula.
segments.s668-g182: که here introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause 'that/so that'. زاینده is used as 'producer/begetter' (one who gives birth or begets). را is the definite object marker attached to زاینده. برتو is the prepositional expression 'upon you' (بر + تو). باید is the impersonal/modal 'must/ought', and گریست is the infinitive/past-stem used as a verbal noun 'to weep/one should weep' — together: 'that a begetter should weep upon you' (i.e., people should lament you).
segments.s668-g183: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'gave such an answer' (چنین = thus, داد = gave, پاسخ = answer). که introduces the quoted content. من is the first-person pronoun 'I'. جادوام is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'I am of sorcery' or 'I am a magician/sorcerer' — here it functions as a predicative identifying the speaker.
segments.s668-g184: ز (poetic from از) introduces origin or source 'of/from'. مردی 'man/manhood' and و connects the next phrase. از مردمی 'from/of the people' uses مردمی as 'of the folk/people'. یک‌سوام is an idiomatic quantifying expression 'one-part / one among many' — grammatically it qualifies the speaker's relation to those groups: 'I am but a single part of men and of peoples.'
segments.s668-g121: This is a compact nominal phrase with a past verb: 'سر ساوه آمد بخاک اندرون' = 'the head of Saveh came/fell to the ground inside'. Word order is Subject (سر ساوه) + verb (آمد) + circumstantial phrase (بخاک اندرون). 'سر' here is literal 'head' and 'ساوه' is a place-name in the genitive relationship (head of Saveh), while آمد is the simple past 'came/fell'.
segments.s668-g122: This clause describes result/location: 'بزیر اندرش خاک شد جوی خون' literally 'down beneath it the earth became a stream of blood.' 'بزیر' places the action downward, 'اندرش' = 'in/within it' with the enclitic -ش 'its', 'خاک شد' is the copular structure meaning 'the earth became', and 'جوی خون' is the predicative noun phrase 'a stream of blood.'
segments.s668-g123: Narrative past with verbless implication: 'شد آن نامور شاه و چندان سپاه' uses شد in an episodic sense ('it happened/there was') to introduce loss: 'that famed king and so many troops (came to be lost/are gone)'. Word order places the verb شد first for narrative force, then the definite demonstrative آن plus the noun phrases; چندان qualifies سپاه as 'so many/much'.
segments.s668-g124: A nominal apposition describing what was associated with them: 'همان تخت زرین و زرین کلاه' = 'that same golden throne and golden helmet.' 'همان' is the demonstrative 'that/the same', followed by two coordinated noun phrases (تخت زرین و زرین کلاه) linked by و.
segments.s668-g125: 'چنینست' is a fused copular expression meaning 'such is' or 'it is thus', followed by the noun phrase 'کردار گردان سپهر' ('the deed/work of the revolving sky'). گردان here is adjectival/participial meaning 'revolving' (describing سپهر), and the phrase as a whole states a general observation about fate/heaven.
segments.s668-g126: A balanced negative clause: 'نه نامهربانیش پیدا نه مهر' = 'neither its unkindness is apparent, nor (its) favour.' The first نه negates 'نامهربانیش پیدا' ('its unkindness is visible'), the repetition of نه begins the second arm where مهر ('favour/affection') stands in contrast; the verb 'is' is omitted (understood) after مهر, a common poetic ellipsis.
segments.s668-g127: 'نگر' is an imperative 'look/heed!'. 'تا' introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that / lest', and 'ننازی' is a negative-inflected verb meaning 'do not act proud/be coy' (poetic form where ن- negates). 'به تخت بلند' is a locative phrase 'at/to the lofty throne.' The structure warns against pride before the high throne.
segments.s668-g128: 'چو' = 'when' introduces the temporal clause; 'ایمن شوی' is a simple present-subjunctive or present-future sense 'when you become safe' with شوی as the 2nd-person singular of شدن; 'دورباش' is an imperative/advice 'be distant/stay away', and 'از گزند' is a prepositional phrase 'from harm.' Together it gives the practical counsel to keep away once you are secure.
segments.s668-g337: بیاورد is a narrative past (3rd person) meaning “he brought.” زان پس is a temporal sequence meaning “after that / then” (زان = “that/of that”; پس = “after/then”). صد و سی هزار is the numeral phrase “one hundred and thirty thousand” where صد و سی = 130 and هزار multiplies it to thousands; the conjunction و simply links the hundreds and tens.
segments.s668-g338: ز introduces origin (“from / out of”) and گنجی is the noun “treasure.” که begins a relative clause (“that/which”) and بود is the past copula “was.” از پدر is a standard prepositional phrase “from (his) father,” and یادگار is the noun “memento/keepsake.” The whole relative clause modifies گنجی: “the treasure that was a memento from his father.”
segments.s668-g339: سه یک (literally “three one”) here means “one of three” or a third share. زان نخستین ties it to the previously mentioned portion (“that first [share]”). بدرویش is an archaic/fused form here functioning as ‘to the dervish’ (equivalent to به درویش); داد is the past tense “gave.”
segments.s668-g340: پرستندگان را is the definite/object-marked noun phrase “the worshippers” (را marks the direct object/topic). درم بیش داد literally “gave dirhams more,” i.e. he gave the worshippers extra dirhams — درم = dirham/coin, بیش = “more / additional,” and داد = “gave.” Word order here places the recipient with را first, then the amount, then the verb.
segments.s668-g341: سه یک دیگر = “another one of the three” (another third). از بهر is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning “for / on behalf of,” here marking purpose or destination. آتشکده is the noun “fire‑temple.” The phrase says that one other share was allocated for the fire‑temple.
segments.s668-g342: همان بهر = “that same for,” i.e. specifying the intended use. نوروز و جشن سده are two festivals: نوروز (Nowruz) and جشن سده (the Sadeh festival). و coordinates the two festival nouns; the clause explicates that the share was intended for those celebrations.
segments.s668-g343: فرستاد is past tense “he sent.” تا + verb in the subjunctive/ present-tense form (دهند) expresses purpose (“so that / in order that”). هیربد را is the object of دهند — “to the hirbad” (هیربد = chief priest); را marks it as the definite recipient. دهند is the subjunctive/plural present of دادن used in a purpose clause: “they may give / they should give.”
segments.s668-g344: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining the purpose/result. در پیش = “in front of / before” (prepositional phrase) and آتشکده is the location “the fire‑temple.” برنهند is a subjunctive/plural verb meaning “they place / they set (upon).” Together the clause says they should place (the gifts) before the fire‑temple.
segments.s668-g425: "اگر" introduces a conditional clause. Here the clause reads literally 'if (a) friend be the Creator of the World' — Persian allows the predicate "یار" ('ally/friend') to appear before the subject "جهان‌آفرین"; "باشد" is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used in the protasis ('if ... be/is').
segments.s668-g426: "به خون پدر" marks the object/purpose ('for the blood of (my) father'); "خواهم" is first-person ('I will') functioning as the main verb meaning 'I will claim/seek/exact' in this context. "از کوه کین" uses "از" to mark source ('from') and places the genitive relation with "کین" after "کوه" in the idiomatic poetic order ('mountain of vengeance').
segments.s668-g427: "بدان‌گه" is a temporal phrase 'at that time / on that occasion' and "که" introduces the subordinate clause ('when/that'). In the clause "بهرام شد جنگ‌جوی", "شد" is the past/copulative 'became', linking subject "بهرام" with predicate "جنگ‌جوی" (i.e. 'Bahram became a warrior').
segments.s668-g428: "از ایران" = 'from Iran' ("از" = source). "سوی ترک" = 'toward the Turk(s)' ("سوی" = toward). The verb phrase "بنهاد روی" is a classical compound meaning 'he set/placed his face' i.e. 'he turned his face / headed' — "بنهاد" is the past form of نهادن ('put/set') and "روی" ('face') is its object, so together 'directed his face toward...'.
segments.s668-g385: This is a nominal clause: subject noun + past-tense verb. دزی (a robber/thief) is the head noun, followed by داشت (past 'had'), so literally 'He had a thief'. پرموده is an adjectival/participle 'experienced' modifying the thief, and افراز نام (‘called Afraz’) is a name-tag construction where نام ('name') follows the name-word (here put after افراز).
segments.s668-g386: کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from whom/whence' linking to the previous noun (the thief). دز here refers again to a thief/action of stealing and functions as the oblique actor in the relative clause. بدی is a noun meaning 'badness/evil' but here best read as 'evil/deeds' associated with theft; ایمن و شادکام are a coordinated adjectival predicate 'safe and prosperous/joyful' describing the state connected to that thief (i.e., the thief’s actions left him secure and fortunate).
segments.s668-g387: نهاد is a past tense verb 'placed/put'. آنچ 'that which/what' is the direct object; بودش functions as an auxiliary/enclitic meaning 'that which had been' or 'that which was (his)', and بدز is the verb 'to steal' (here in a verbal adjective/participle sense 'stolen'). در درم is a locative phrase 'into the dirham/treasury' with در giving 'in/into' and درم ('dirham') used here for money/treasury.
segments.s668-g388: This is a short list introduced by the preposition ز 'of/from' (poetic use meaning 'consisting of' here): دینار 'dinar(s)' and گوهر 'gems/jewels' connected by و 'and'. بیش و کم is a fixed pair meaning 'more and less' (i.e., approximately, varying amounts). The ordering is typical Persian: preposition + items + closing quantifier.
segments.s668-g389: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; جیحون is the proper name (the Amu Darya river). گذر کرد is past 'crossed/passed' (گذر as noun + کرد as light verb yields 'made passage'), and خود is emphatic 'himself' placed before با سپاه 'with (his) army' which is an adjunct indicating accompaniment.
segments.s668-g390: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came'. گرازان is the plural noun 'boars' (here either literal or a poetic troop-image). سوی is the preposition 'toward', and زرم‌گاه is a compound 'treasury/place of wealth' serving as the goal of motion.
segments.s668-g391: دو لشکر is the subject 'two armies'. به تنگ اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came into the narrow (pass)' (به تنگ = into the narrow place, اندر آمد = entered). The trailing به جنگ 'for battle' states purpose/intent — they entered the pass in order to fight.
segments.s668-g392: This clause negates an action: به‌ره بر نکردند literally 'they did not take/bring spoils' (به‌ره بر = take a share/spoil; بر کردن = to take up). جایی درنگ 'anywhere delay' is a negated locative adverbial: جایی 'any place/anywhere' + درنگ 'pause/delay', so together 'they did not stay/linger anywhere.'
segments.s668-g129: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' placed before the clause. بهرام is the subject (proper name) followed by the epithet جنگی 'warlike/warrior' modifying the name. رسید is a simple past verb 'arrived/reached'. اندروی is an adverbial (postverbal here) meaning 'inward(ly)' and indicates direction or manner of the arrival.
segments.s668-g130: کشیدش is past 'drew/dragged' with the attached object clitic -ش 'him/it' (referring back to someone mentioned). بر آن خاک is a prepositional phrase 'onto that earth/ground' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, خاک = ground). تفته is an adjectival descriptor of خاک (poetic; 'trod up/scattered' here) and بروی is a motion verb 'went/was brought onto' giving the sense of placing/dragging onto the ground.
segments.s668-g131: برید is a past verb meaning 'they cut/severed' (here used transitively). آن سر is 'that head' (آن = that, سر = head). شاه‌وارش is a compound with the adjectival/possessive sense 'kingly / his kingly [head]' (شاه‌وار + -ش), and ز تن is the prepositional phrase 'from the body' (ز = from, تن = body).
segments.s668-g132: نیامد is negative past 'did not come' / in context idiomatically 'no one came'. کسی here is quantified under the negation and should be understood as 'anyone' / 'no one' depending on negation. پیشش is a locative 'before him / to his presence' and از انجمن 'from the assembly' specifies the group who did not come.
segments.s668-g133: چوترکان is a plural substantive 'the Turk-like people' (an epithet or comparison). رسیدند is past plural 'they arrived/reached'. نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near/close' and شاه is the object/goal 'the king' — together: 'they came near the king.'
segments.s668-g134: فگنده تنی is a nominal phrase: فگنده (a participial/adjectival past form 'thrown/scattered') modifies تنی 'a body' (corporeal). بود is the copula 'was'. بی‌سر is an adjective 'headless'. به راه is a locative phrase 'on the road' (به = on/toward, راه = road). Word order places descriptive adjectives before/after the noun in a poetic, compressed way.
segments.s668-g135: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. برگرفتند is past plural 'they took up/raised' (here 'raised a cry'). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once' modifying برگرفتند, and خروش is the direct object 'a cry/uproar' — together: 'everyone raised at once a cry.'
segments.s668-g136: زمین پر خروش is a verbless/nominal clause 'the earth full of clamor' (زمین = earth, پر = full (of), خروش = clamor). و links the clauses; هوا پر ز جوش is parallel 'the air full of surge/boiling' (هوا = air, پر = full (of), ز = of/from, جوش = boiling/surge). The construction uses پر + noun to mean 'full of X.'
segments.s668-g17: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (gaze/watch) plus کرد (past of کردن) = 'kept watch' or 'looked'. زان (from that) precedes the noun phrase رزمگه ساوه شاه (battlefield of Saveh — شاه 'the king' follows the place name), so the line means 'the king of Saveh looked at/kept watch over that battlefield.' Word order: verb + (prepositional/locative phrase) + nominal identifier ('ساوه شاه').
segments.s668-g18: که introduces a subordinate clause ('for/that') giving the reason. آن جادویی را uses آن ('that') + جادویی (magic/sorcery) with را as the definite object marker. ندادند is the past-tense negative of دادن ('they did not give/allow') and takes راه ('way, passage') as the thing not given — راه here means 'a way/passage' (i.e. to let it through). The clause says they did not allow that sorcery a way.
segments.s668-g19: بیاورد is past tense 'brought' (third-person) used here to report a movement. لشکر (army) is the direct object/affected noun and سوی (toward) marks direction. میسره follows as the destination/quality of the place ('accessible/reachable' in the given sense list), so the phrase means 'he brought the army toward the reachable spot.'
segments.s668-g20: چو introduces a simile ('as/like'). گرگ اندر آمد is a short clause 'a wolf came in/among', with اندر ('in/among') plus آمد (past 'came'). به‌پیش بره ('before/unto the lamb') places the wolf's action relative to the lamb; the whole compares the approach to a wolf approaching a lamb.
segments.s668-g21: چویک ('as one') is an adverbial phrase modifying the following clause. روی لشکر here is the front/face of the army (روی 'face/surface' modifying لشکر), and به‌هم برشکست is the verbal phrase: به‌هم ('together') + برشکست (past 'broke/shattered'). The line means the army, united as one, broke (i.e., gave way or shattered) collectively.
segments.s668-g22: سوی قلبِ بهرام is a directional phrase ('toward Bahram's heart'). یازید is a past verb meaning 'broke forth / gaped / thrust forward' (here used with دست as the actor), and دست ('hand') is the noun that either performs or is affected by that motion; Persian allows this noun to appear after the verb for poetic effect. The clause says a hand sprang forth/toward Bahram's heart.
segments.s668-g23: نگه کرد (kept watch/looked) is again the light-verb construction: نگه (gaze) + کرد (did). بهرام is the subject placed between the verb elements for emphasis. زان قلب‌گاه ('from that heart-seat') is a locative/figurative phrase specifying the vantage point or the inner place he surveyed.
segments.s668-g24: گریزان is a present-participial adjective 'fleeing' that modifies سپه ('troops/army'); دید is the past verb 'he saw'. پیش سپاه ('before the host') is a prepositional phrase placing where/relative to whom he saw them — 'fleeing troops before the host.' The order is participle + noun (subject) + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s668-g25: بیامد is the simple past narrative verb ('he came') used to introduce the action. به‌نیزه is an instrumental/ablative phrase ('with a spear') formed by the preposition به attached to نیزه (poetic clitic spelling). سه تن را is the definite direct object ('three persons') marked by را, and ز زین uses the poetic preposition ز (modern از) + زین 'saddle' to indicate 'from their saddle(s)'; the whole phrase indicates he came up with a spear and (affected) three men from their saddles.
segments.s668-g26: نگون‌سار کرد combines the adjective نگون‌سار ('downcast/overthrown') with کرد ('made/caused') so literally 'he made (them) downcast' — a poetic causative meaning 'he overthrew/ruined them'. The conjunction و links to بزد, the past tense 'he struck', and بر زمین is a prepositional phrase ('onto/upon the ground') describing where he struck them.
segments.s668-g27: همی‌گفت is an imperfective/habitual past form meaning 'he kept saying' (poetic continuous past). زین سان is an idiomatic classical phrase 'in this manner/thus' (زین here is archaic/poetic), and بود کارزار places the copula بود ('was') after the phrase to state 'the battle was thus' — a typical older Persian word order with the copula following the predicate.
segments.s668-g28: همین بود رسم combines the demonstrative همین ('this, the same') with the past copula بود to mean 'this was the custom.' The coordinating و links to a parallel clause همین بود کار where the same structure repeats, using همین ... بود as a formulaic repetition ('this was the custom and this was the practice').
segments.s668-g29: ندارید is 2nd-person plural present/gnomic negative 'you do not have' (or 'you have no'). شرم is the object 'shame.' از introduces the locus/standard ('before/regarding'), and خدای جهان is a genitive phrase 'God of the world' (an epithet for God); the whole clause reads 'You have no shame before God of the world.'
segments.s668-g30: نه negates the following phrase ('nor'). از again marks the relation 'before/of.' نامداران is a plural substantive/adjective 'the renowned (ones).' فرخ and مهان are epithets; here they qualify the named people as 'fortunate/auspicious nobles/guests' — a classical compound used to praise notable men. The phrase continues the list of those before whom they show no shame.
segments.s668-g31: و is the simple conjunction 'and.' زان پس is a classical sequence marker 'after that' (زان 'from/for that' + پس 'then/after'). بیامد is the past narrative 'he came.' سوی میمنه is a directional phrase with سوی 'toward' and میمنه 'the right (side)' — here indicating the direction he moved.
segments.s668-g32: چو is a comparative/temporal conjunction 'like/as' (often 'as' or 'when' in poetic usage). شیر is 'lion.' ژیان here is an archaic adjective/noun related to 'life' (literally 'living' or 'full of life') modifying شیر ('a living/fierce lion'). کو in older Persian can function as a relative/particle (here introducing the subordinate time-clause 'when/that it becomes'); شود is the present/future-forming verb 'becomes,' and گرسنه is the predicate adjective 'hungry.' The clause therefore reads 'like a (living) lion when it becomes hungry.'
segments.s668-g321: This is a nominal phrase naming and describing a person: 'انوشه بدی' functions as a proper name (compound) followed by two adjectives 'شاد و رامش‌پذیر' ('happy and tractable'). There is no copula; in Persian poetry such adjective phrases immediately follow the name to qualify it.
segments.s668-g322: 'که' introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding name. 'بخت بد اندیش' is a noun phrase 'ill‑thinking / ill‑wishing Fate' (بخت = fate, بد اندیش = ill‑minded); 'توگشت' is an archaic fused form meaning 'تو گشت' = 'you became' (past), and 'پیر' is the predicate 'old'. Together the clause means 'whose ill‑minded fate made you old.'
segments.s668-g323: A nominal/predicative clause: 'سر ساوه شاهست' = 'the chief/head of Saveh is king' (سر + place; شاهست = 'is king'). The conjunction 'و' links an additional nominal phrase 'کهتر پسر' = 'the younger (or lesser) son' which continues into the next clause.
segments.s668-g324: 'که' again begins a relative clause modifying 'پسر'. 'فغفور' names the emperor; 'خواندیش' is an archaic past verb phrase containing an enclitic object ('ایش' = 'him') meaning 'called him'; 'وی‌را پدر' places the complement after the verb: 'called him (وی‌را) father (پدر)'. The structure is therefore 'whom the Faghfūr called father.'
segments.s668-g325: 'زده' is a past participle here meaning 'set' or 'placed'. It takes the prepositional phrase 'بر سرنیزه‌ها' ('on the spear‑tips') and the adverbial 'بر درست' ('upright/erect' — a poetic locative), so the whole phrase describes something set up on the points of spears in an upright manner.
segments.s668-g326: 'همه شهر' is the subject 'the whole city'; 'نظاره' functions as a verbal noun meaning 'the gazing / observation' (used here like a verb); 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that'; 'سرست' is an archaic fusion of 'سر است' meaning 'that is the head' or simply 'that head'. Together the clause says 'the whole city gazed at that head' (literally: 'the whole city — the gazing — that head').
segments.s668-g327: 'شهنشاه' is the subject 'the king of kings'; 'بشنید' is the simple past 'he heard'; the phrase 'بر پای خاست' is an idiom meaning 'he rose to his feet' (بر پای = 'on foot', خاست = past of 'to rise'). Word order is subject — verb — prepositional phrase.
segments.s668-g328: 'بزودی' is an adverb 'quickly/soon'; 'خم آورد' is a compound past tense meaning 'he bent' or 'he bowed' (خم = bend, آورد = brought/made); 'بالای راست' is a directional/locative phrase 'to the right side' or 'on the right' indicating the direction of the bending. The whole gives a quick action following the rising.
segments.s668-g225: که is a relative pronoun linking this descriptive clause to the person named earlier ('who'). فرخ نژادی is an adjectival phrase where فرخ ('fortunate') qualifies نژادی ('by descent'), literally 'fortunate by birth/lineage' (no copula is used). The coordinated phrase و فرخ سری repeats فرخ ('fortunate') with سری ('head/chief') used metaphorically for rank, intellect, or good fortune of the head.
segments.s668-g226: ستون is a predicative noun meaning 'pillar' or 'support' describing the person; همه ('all') scopes over the following coordinated nouns. شهر و بوم و بری are three coordinated geographic terms ('city, homeland, and region/wilderness') forming the domain of which he is the pillar; word order places the general modifier before the list.
segments.s668-g227: پراگنده گشتند is a past-tense plural intransitive construction: گشتند ('became/were') with the adjective پراگنده ('scattered/widespread') yields 'they were scattered' or 'they became dispersed.' ز آوردگاه uses the poetic ablative ز ('from') to mark origin — 'from the battlefield.'
segments.s668-g228: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones' (plural). و هم is a coordination with هم reinforcing 'also/likewise.' پهلوان سپاه is a genitival/nominal phrase 'champions (pahlavān) of the army/host' (پهلوان modifying or being related to سپاه to mean the army's champions).
segments.s668-g229: شب تیره ('dark night') is the subject/scene-setting noun phrase. چون here means 'as' or 'when.' زلف را تاب داد is a causative-style clause: تاب ('twist/sway') is the object given by داد ('gave') and را marks زلف as the definite object — literally 'the dark night gave a twist to the lock (of hair).' تاب is used poetically for a curl or sweep.
segments.s668-g230: همان تاب ('that same twist') resumes the effect just mentioned. او is a third-person pronoun referring to the beloved/that source of the twist; چشم را خواب داد is a causative phrase 'gave sleep to the eye' (چشم marked with را as definite object), i.e. 'lulled the eye to sleep.'
segments.s668-g231: پدید آمد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'appeared' or 'came into view' (پدید 'manifest' + آمد 'came'). آن پرده آبنوس is a definite noun phrase 'that ebony curtain/veil' with آبنوس as a descriptive adjective ('ebony/very dark').
segments.s668-g232: بر آسود گیتی is an inverted clause: گیتی ('the world') is described as آسود ('at peace, rested'); بر is a prepositional/idiomatic particle here linking the state. ز آواز کوس uses the poetic ablative ز ('from/because of') with آواز کوس ('the sound/voice of the drum') — 'the world was at rest (or found rest) from the sound of the drum.'
segments.s668-g201: زمانی is a temporal adverb placed sentence‑initially meaning 'once/formerly'. همی‌گفت is an imperfective/habitual past-form verb (poetic) meaning 'would say' or 'kept saying'; here it frames repeated speech. برساوه شاه is a noun phrase 'the king at/ of Saveh' with برساوه a locative/relational place-name placed before شاه (poetic inversion places the subject after the verb).
segments.s668-g202: چه سود آمد is an interrogative clause 'what profit/benefit came' with آمد (past) as the main verb and چه introducing the question. ازجادویی is a prepositional phrase 'from/by sorcery' (از + جادویی), and برسپاه is a locative/target phrase 'upon/against the army' completing the clause.
segments.s668-g203: همه نیکویها is a plural noun phrase 'all the good/excellent things' (نیکوی + plural ـها). ز یزدان بود uses poetic ز for 'from' plus یزدان 'God' and بود as the past copula 'was', so the clause states origin: 'all good things were from God.'
segments.s668-g204: کسی را کجا بخت خندان بود is a rhetorical/existential question. کسی را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker attached to 'someone/anyone'; کجا 'where' introduces the locational/interrogative sense. بخت خندان is a noun + adjective 'a smiling/fortunate fate' and بود is the past copula 'was' (i.e. 'where was there anyone whose fortune smiled?').
segments.s668-g205: بفرمود is a classical/honorific perfect-past verb used here to report a command ('he ordered/said'); it is followed by a verbal-noun clause functioning as object: از تن بریدن سرش literally 'the cutting of his head from the body.' بریدن is the verbal noun 'to cut/behead' and از marks separation from the body; ش is the third‑person possessive clitic 'his.'
segments.s668-g206: جدا کرد is an active perfect/past 'separated/did separate' with جان ('life/soul') as its direct object and از تن marking the source ('from the body'). بی‌برش is a descriptive adjective after the clause meaning 'ill‑fated' (lit. 'without offspring' in some senses) and modifies the person whose life was taken; the subject (those who executed the order) is implicit.
segments.s668-g207: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. او را بکشتند (here written as رابکشتند) is object (او را) + past plural verb 'they killed', which follows the object (typical Persian order with را-marked object). بر پای خاست is an idiomatic phrase 'stood up to his feet' with بر + پای and the past verb خاست 'rose/stood up.'
segments.s668-g208: چنین گفت 'thus he said' places چنين ('thus') before گفت (said). کای is the vocative particle 'O', addressing داور 'Judge/Arbiter'. داد و راست are two nominal nouns 'justice (داد) and right/uprightness (راست)' forming the petition; Persian often strings nouns as a plea without an explicit verb in reported speech.
segments.s668-g409: شمار سپاهش پدیدار نیست is a nominal phrase + predicate: شمار سپاه + possessive suffix ش (= 'his') yields 'the number/size of his army'. پدیدار نیست is a negated stative predicate meaning 'is not manifest/visible'. Word order is subject (شمار سپاهش) then predicate (پدیدار نیست), typical of Persian.
segments.s668-g410: هم این رزم را کس خریدار نیست is an independent clause with topic/emphatic marker را after رزم. هم here functions as an emphatic/limiting particle ('indeed/even'). کس خریدار نیست is a negative existential statement: 'there is no person who is a خریدار (taker/buyer) of this battle' i.e. 'no one will take/accept it.'
segments.s668-g411: سپهدار گردن‌کش و خشمناک is a noun (سپهدار, 'commander') followed by two adjectives (گردن‌کش 'rebellious', خشمناک 'angry/wrathful') joined by و. Adjectives follow the noun in the expected Persian order and together describe the commander.
segments.s668-g412: همی خون شود زیر او تیره خاک contains a verb phrase with poetic auxiliary/marker همی + شود (become/happen) producing a future/habitual sense ('will come to pass / blood will be shed'). زیر او is a locative/relative phrase 'under him' and تیره خاک is the predicate nominal 'the earth dark' (the earth is darkened). The sequence expresses result: blood will be shed and the ground beneath him will turn dark.
segments.s668-g413: چو شب تیره گردد is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو ('when') + subject شب ('night') + predicate تیره گردد ('becomes dark'). Shibīkhun کنیم uses کنیم (1st plural subjunctive/optative) to express an intended action 'we will make/conduct a night-raid' once the condition is met.
segments.s668-g414: ز دل بیم و اندیشه بیرون کنیم is a transitive verbal phrase where ز دل ('from the heart') marks the source, followed by the coordinated objects بیم و اندیشه ('fear and thought/worry'). بیرون کنیم ('we will put/drive out') is a compound verb meaning 'expel' or 'cast out'. Word order places the source phrase before the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s668-g415: چو پرموده آمد به پرده سرای is a temporal clause: چو ('when') + modifier پرموده ('skilled/seasoned') modifying an omitted personal subject, then آمد ('came'). به پرده سرای is a locative phrase 'to the curtained chamber/curtain-place' (پرده as 'curtain', سرای here used in the sense of a place/performative setting), so the whole means 'when the experienced one came to the curtained chamber.'
segments.s668-g416: همی‌زد ز هر گونه از جنگ رای has همی‌زد (imperfect/habitual 'kept saying/putting forth') as the verb, followed by ز هر گونه از جنگ رای ('from every kind of war, counsel/plan'). رای here means 'plan/counsel', so the phrase means he continually offered counsels/strategies of every kind regarding warfare. The preposition ز introduces the source/material of the counsels.
segments.s668-g233: همی‌گشت is the imperfective/continuous poetic form (همی + گشتن) meaning 'kept turning' or 'was turning.' گردون is the subject ('the vault of heaven / sky') placed after the verb, as is common in elevated/classical Persian. شتاب آمدش is a small clause where شتاب (a noun 'haste') is followed by آمدش ('came upon it/him'), yielding the idiom 'haste came upon (it/him)' — i.e. it/he hurried.
segments.s668-g234: شب تیره is a noun + attributive adjective ('the dark night'); را is the definite object/contrast marker highlighting that phrase. دیریاب آمدش is an adjectival/verb sequence: دیریاب ('long-coming / rare' in archaic/poetic use) + آمدش ('came upon him/it'), so together 'the long-coming (or hard-to-find) dark night came upon him.'
segments.s668-g235: بر آمد is the compound verbal idea 'rose up / came up' (بر used as a verbal preposition/prefix with آمدن). یکی here means 'a certain' (indefinite), زرد کشتی is the noun phrase 'yellow boat,' and ز آب is the ablative prepositional phrase 'from the water' (poetic z = از). Word order is Verb – Indefinite NP – Adjective – Noun – Source, typical of older/fixed poetic word order.
segments.s668-g236: بپالود is a past-tense/heavy-aspect verb form (بـ + پالودن) meaning 'purified/dispelled'; it is repeated for parallelism. رنج and خواب are the two direct objects linked by و ('and'): 'it dispelled sorrow and dispelled sleep.' The repeated verbal form gives a rhythmic, emphatic effect.
segments.s668-g237: سپهبد is the title/agent ('the spahbed,' a high-ranking commander). بیامد is the past tense 'came.' فرستاد کس is a transitive clause 'sent someone' (فرستاد followed by the indefinite object کس). The sequence gives: 'The spahbed came and sent someone.'
segments.s668-g238: به‌نزدیک یاران is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the companions' indicating destination/position (به‌نزدیک = 'to/near'). فریادرس follows as an appositive/role-name: the person sent is described as فریادرس ('a rescuer, succorer'). In Persian the role noun often follows the destination phrase, giving 'sent someone to the companions — a rescuer.'
segments.s668-g239: که تا introduces a subordinate clause (classical/poetic use where que + ta give a purposive/resultive sense). هرک is an indefinite relative 'whoever'; شد + کشته is a periphrastic passive/result ('became killed' = 'was slain'), with شد functioning as auxiliary. از مهتران marks the group 'from/of the chiefs' (از = 'among/of'), so this clause reads 'that whoever was slain among the chiefs...'.
segments.s668-g240: بزرگان ترکان و جنگ آوران is a coordinated apposition elaborating مهتران: 'the great ones (بزرگان) — the Turks (ترکان) and the war-bringers/warriors (جنگ‌آوران).' بزرگان is the head noun 'great ones/nobles,' followed by the ethnic/designation and a coordinated noun phrase; this continues the descriptive list started in the previous segment.
segments.s668-g1: چنین گفت پس is a poetic ordering meaning “thus he said, then.” با + NP marks accompaniment or addressee; here با سپه ساوه شاه addresses/mentions the king of Saveh together with his army (poetic omission of ezāfe/connector). The sequence places the speech header before a subordinate clause introduced by که.
segments.s668-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause (“that/which”). از جادوی = “by/through magic” (از marks the agent/means). اندر آرید راه is a reverse poetic word order: راه is the object, اندر (in) qualifies the action, and آرید is an archaic/poetic form of آوردن (‘bring/make’), here meaning ‘cause/bring about’ (they caused by magic).
segments.s668-g3: بدان is the imperative/hortative ‘know/remember (this)’ composed of the prefix ب- + دان. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here (‘so that’). دل و چشم ایرانیان is the compound subject (‘the hearts and eyes of the Iranians’).
segments.s668-g4: بپیچد (subjunctive/optative form of پیچیدن) expresses the hoped-for/prevented result ‘(will) not be turned/disturbed.’ نیاید is negative optative/old-form ‘may not come/shall not come,’ and شما را زیان uses را as the object marker on شما with زیان ‘harm’ as the predicate object: ‘that harm not come to you.’ The clause expresses purpose/result.
segments.s668-g5: همه is the plural subject ‘all/everyone.’ جاودان is an adjective ‘everlasting’ modifying جادوی ‘magic/sorcery’ (poetic -ی form). ساختند is past plural ‘they made/fashioned.’ The sequence states an action performed collectively.
segments.s668-g6: همی is a frequent epic adverb (here ‘then/and so’ or continuous sense). در هوا is a locative phrase ‘into/within the air/sky,’ آتش is the object ‘fire,’ and انداختند is past plural ‘they threw/cast’ — together: ‘they cast fire into the air.’
segments.s668-g7: برآمد is a past tense verb ‘arose/issued forth.’ یکی is an indefinite article-like word ‘a certain’ (here introducing an event). باد و ابری سیاه lists two phenomena, ‘a wind and a black cloud’ (ابری used as noun ‘a cloud’ + سیاه ‘black’).
segments.s668-g8: همی again is the epic adverb ‘then/indeed.’ تیر بارید is a figurative phrasing ‘arrows rained’ (تیر ‘arrows’ + بارید ‘rained/fell’). ازو ‘from it’ and بر سپاه ‘upon the army’ give source and target of the falling arrows.
segments.s668-g257: نخستین (adjective) precedes سر (noun) to form 'the foremost/first head'. ساوه is a genitive qualifier (the head of Saveh). برنیزه کرد is a verbal phrase: برنیزه (lit. 'on a spear') + کرد (past tense 3rd singular of کردن) = 'he put/placed (it) on a spear'. This is a simple past narrative clause.
segments.s668-g258: درفشی (indefinite noun) 'a banner/standard' is the object; کجا is the interrogative 'where'; داشتی is 2nd-person past (or past-imperfect) 'you had' — here used to ask where the addressee's banner was; در نبرد is a locative prepositional phrase 'in battle' placed after the verb phrase. Order: object + question word + verb + locative.
segments.s668-g259: سران 'leaders' is the head noun; بزرگان 'the great ones' modifies it in a genitive/attributive chain, so 'leaders of the great men'; توران زمین is another noun phrase in apposition/qualification meaning 'the land of Turan'. Persian stacks modifiers right-to-left: سرانِ بزرگانِ تورانِ زمین = 'the leaders of the great men of the land of Turan.'
segments.s668-g260: چنان هم 'likewise/such too' is an adverbial phrase; درفش سواران is a genitive construct 'the banner of the riders'; چین is a noun 'fold(s)' used predicatively — the clause is elliptical (no explicit verb) and effectively says 'the riders' banner likewise had such folds.' Word order: adverbial → possessor phrase → predicate noun.
segments.s668-g261: بفرمود is the courteous/past perfective of گفتن/فرمودن 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces a subordinate (purpose/result) clause: 'that ...'. برستور is an adverbial phrase meaning 'on horseback / mounted on steeds' modifying the action in the subordinate clause. نوند here appears as a noun or name (contextually likely a group/name tied to the action); its precise role is as the element tied into what was to be brought or who was to be mounted.
segments.s668-g262: به‌زودی is an adverb 'soon/at once'; برشاه ایران is a prepositional/goal phrase 'to/upon the king of Iran' (برشاه = toward the king); برند is the verbal form (3rd person plural) 'they carry/bring' (here governed by تا: 'that they bring'). Combined: 'that they be brought at once to the king of Iran.'
segments.s668-g263: اسیران 'the captives' is a noun phrase; و links to the following noun phrase. آن خواسته 'that request/demand' (آن = that; خواسته = wish/request). هرچ بود 'whatever it was' — هرچ (whatever/however many) + بود (past 'was'). Together: 'the captives, and whatever that request was.'
segments.s668-g264: همی‌داشت is a past habitual/continuous form (همی = archaic progressive prefix + داشت) meaning 'he kept/was holding'; اندر هری is locative 'in/within Hari' (a region/river); نابسود is an adjective/adverb 'fruitless / to no avail' qualifying the action. Literal order: 'he kept ... in Hari, fruitlessly.'
segments.s668-g137: "پسر گفت" is a simple subject + past verb: 'the son said'. "پسر" is the subject (noun). "گفت" is the past tense of 'to say' used here to introduce reported speech; the lemma senses provided do not include the verbal sense 'to say', so treat it as the past-reporting verb that links to the following clause.
segments.s668-g138: This is a subordinate (reported) clause introduced by relative/conjunction "که" = 'that'. Word order is emphatic: "بهرام را بخت بیدار بود" literally 'to Bahram fortune awake was' = 'Bahram's fortune was awake.' "را" marks the noun phrase as definite/affected (here marking the person whose fortune is described). "بخت بیدار بود" is predicate: 'fortune was awake' (past).
segments.s668-g139: "ز تنگی" = 'because of the narrowness' (ز as a poetic 'from/because of' + تنگی 'narrowness'). "کجا راه بد بر سپاه" is a relative/descriptive fragment: "کجا" here = 'where', "راه بد" = 'the road was bad', and "بر سپاه" marks the party affected ('for/to the army'); Persian places affected/beneficiary phrases after the predicate.
segments.s668-g140: "فراوان بمردند" = 'many / plentifully they died' — فراوان functions adverbially. "بمردند" is past plural 'they died'. "زان تنگ راه" = 'in/therefrom that narrow way/pass' (زان = 'from/thereof' pointing back to the narrow place). Word order places the location phrase after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s668-g141: "بسی پیل بسپرد مردم به‌پای": "بسی" here is an archaising quantifier meaning 'many/very' but the provided lemma senses do not include that meaning (so flagged). "پیل" = 'elephants'. "بسپرد" is past 'placed/handed over/assigned' (here 'they deployed/put'). "مردم به‌پای" = 'against the people at (their) feet' or 'people at the feet (of the elephants)' — the prepositional phrase indicates who suffered under/near the elephants.
segments.s668-g142: "نشد" in negative here means 'did not happen / was not the case' (negating existence or occurrence). "زان سپه" = 'of that host/army' (زان = 'from/that', سپه = 'army'). "یکی باز جای" = 'one (a single) free/open place' — یکی 'one', باز 'available/free/open', جای 'place'. Together: 'in that army not a single place remained free.'
segments.s668-g143: "چه" here is an exclamatory 'how/what (a lot)'. "زیر پی پیل" = 'under the foot(s) / tracks of the elephant' — "زیر" 'under', "پی" 'foot/track' (here 'the foot'), "پیل" 'elephant'. "گشته" is a perfect-participle/past participle 'have become / were' used with the adjective "تباه" to form 'were ruined/crushed'.
segments.s668-g144: "چه سرها بریده" again uses exclamatory "چه" = 'how many'; "سرها" plural 'heads'; "بریده" past participle 'cut off'. "به‌آوردگاه" is a compound locative 'to the place of bringing / to the assembly' (i.e., heads brought to the muster/collection point). The clause is a parallel exclamation to the previous one.
segments.s668-g73: به‌پیل اندرون is a prepositional/locative phrase ('on/into the elephant' – به + پیل) followed by تیر باران as a compound where تیر (arrow) is modified by باران used figuratively ('a shower'). گرفت is a past tense verb placed at the end (typical Persian word order) and here has a passive/affected sense 'was struck/was beset'.
segments.s668-g74: کمان را uses را as the definite/object marker on کمان ('the bow'). چو introduces a simile ('like/as'), ابر بهاران is a poetic compound 'spring cloud', and گرفت is a past active verb 'he took/ seized' (subject is omitted and recovered from the surrounding narrative). The verb again appears at the sentence end.
segments.s668-g75: پس is a discourse adverb ('then'). پشت او is a locative/possessive phrase ('behind him / his rear') and اندر آمد is the verbal expression 'came in / approached' with the verb آمد at the end; سپاه (army) is the subject/agent of آمد, placed after the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s668-g76: ستاره شد is past tense 'became (like) stars' (شد = 'became'). از introduces the material/origin ('from' / 'by means of') and the phrase پر و پیکان سیاه lists items ('feathers and black arrows') with the adjective سیاه following the noun it modifies (typical Persian noun–adjective order).
segments.s668-g77: بخستند is a past plural verb (an older/poetic form meaning 'they rose / they lifted') and takes خرطوم پیلان ('the trunks of the elephants') as its object. به‌تیر is an adverbial/agentive phrase ('toward/with arrows' or 'by arrows'), placed after the object in the typical Persian order.
segments.s668-g78: ز خون uses ز ('from' / 'because of') with خون ('blood') as cause or source; شد is the past copula 'became'. در و دشت is a coordinated pair ('gate/door and plain') and چون introduces the simile 'like', with آب‌گیر ('pond') as the comparison. Word order places the verb before its complements in this poetic line.
segments.s668-g79: از آن خستگی is a prepositional phrase ('because of that fatigue' — here از has a causal sense). پشت برگاشتند has پشت ('the rear / back') as the noun and برگاشتند (an older past plural form related to برگشتن) as the verb 'they turned back/retreated'; the verb appears at the end as usual.
segments.s668-g80: بدو is the fused preposition/pronoun ('to it / toward it') referring back to the place; دشت is 'the plain'. پیکار بگذاشتند is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning 'they set/began battle' with بگذاشتند as the past plural verb 'they placed/established'. Word order places the direction (بدو دشت) before the verb phrase.
segments.s668-g65: This is a relative clause introduced by the conjunction که (here = 'who/that'). هرکس که = 'whoever (who)'; بااو ('with him') precedes the verblike predicate کمانست, where کمان‑ست is an older/poetic copular construction (kaman + -st) meaning 'was/is a bow' (i.e. 'had a bow'). The clause ends with و تیر = 'and an arrow', listing the weapons possessed.
segments.s668-g66: کمان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the bow' as definite. بزه is here an element directly following the object (an archaic/poetic word kept in the line); برنهد is the past/preterite 3rd‑person singular of برنهادن 'to place/raise/put on', so 'he put up/raised/took up'. ناگزیر functions as an adverb/adjectival predicate meaning 'inevitably/compelled'. Together: 'he inevitably/takenly put/took up the bow.'
segments.s668-g67: خدنگی (a dagger/short sword) is the head noun followed by a relative clause introduced by که 'whose/that'. پیکانش = 'its arrow' (possessive suffix -ش). یازد is an older poetic form meaning 'gushed/burst forth' (from یازیدن); به‌خون modifies the verb, 'in blood' or 'bloodied'.
segments.s668-g68: This short clause gives the result/location: سه چوبه = 'three beams/shafts' (سه modifies چوبه). به‌خرطوم is a prepositional phrase 'into/at the proboscis/trunk', پیل = 'elephant', and اندرون = 'inside/within'. Word order is noun phrase + locative phrase: 'three shafts into the elephant's trunk within.'
segments.s668-g69: نشانید is a past tense (3pl) verb here meaning 'they set/placed/plant(ed)' (often used of planting a standard or placing something). و = 'and'; پس = 'then/after that'; گرزها is the plural 'maces/clubs' (subject or object depending on verb); برکشید is past 3pl of برکشیدن 'to raise/lift up' — 'they raised the maces.'
segments.s668-g70: به جنگ اندر آیید is a past narrative sequence: به ... جنگ = 'into/for battle', اندر = 'into/among', and آیید (past/poetic 3pl form of آمدن here) = 'they came/entered' — 'they entered into battle'. و = 'and'; دشمن کشید uses دشمن as object/target and کشید (here) is used in the sense of 'slay/strike down' (a killing sense), so 'they struck down the enemy.'
segments.s668-g71: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed/commander'. کمان را (with را) marks 'the bow' as definite object. بزه again appears after the object (archaic/poetic element here) and برنهاد is the past 3rd‑person singular of برنهادن 'to put/place/lay', so 'the commander put/placed the bow (bze)'.
segments.s668-g72: یکی = 'one' (one person / someone). خود adds emphasis 'himself'. پولاد = 'steel' used metonymically for a steel helmet. بر سر = prepositional phrase 'upon the head'; نهاد is past of نهادن 'he placed/put'. Together: 'one himself put a steel [helmet] on his head.'
segments.s668-g105: بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'against them' (بر = 'against/upon' + ایشان = 'them'). یکی quantifies تیر باران: 'یکی تیر باران' = 'a single/one (volley) of arrows' where تیر باران is a figurative compound 'arrow-shower/volley'. کنید is a 2nd-person plural imperative (‘do/let do’), commanding the action.
segments.s668-g106: بکوشید is an imperative (2nd-person plural) 'strive/try'. The conjunction و links to the following clause. کار سواران کنید literally 'do the work of the horsemen' — کار is 'task/work' and سواران is the genitive group 'of the riders/horsemen'; کنید again is the imperative 'do/perform'.
segments.s668-g107: بران is an adverbial/archaic pointer 'upon that' (here marking location). تل بر آمد uses آمد (past simple 'came/rose up') with تل ('mound') as its subject: 'a mound rose up' or 'on the mound there came up'. کجا is a rhetorical 'where?' leading into the identification that follows (ساوه شاه = 'the king of Saveh'). Word order is elevated/poetic: noun + verb then question word then name/title.
segments.s668-g108: همی‌بود is a past-continuous copular form 'was / kept being' (descriptive past). بر تخت زر is the locative phrase 'on a golden throne' (بر = 'on', تخت = 'throne', زر = 'gold'). با کلاه 'with a hat/crown' uses با = 'with' to mark accompaniment; کلاه here denotes the crown/ceremonial hat.
segments.s668-g109: و is the narrative conjunction 'and/then'. را is the definite-object marker (here marking the person seen, which is contextually supplied). دید is past 'saw/beheld'. برتازیی is a noun from تازیدن meaning 'dashing/charging prowess' functioning as descriptive complement ('in his dashing'); چون هزبر is a simile 'like Hezbar.'
segments.s668-g110: همی‌تاخت is past-continuous 'was charging' or 'rushed' (imperfective past). در دشت is the locative phrase 'in/over the plain'. برسان ابر is an idiomatic simile: برسان + ابر = 'like a cloud' (literally 'bringing/like a cloud'), so the clause means he swept across the plain 'as a cloud' — a poetic image of a dense, sweeping mass.
segments.s668-g111: خدنگی گزین کرد is a verb phrase 'he chose/select(ed) a khadang' (خدنگی = khadang, a short spear or dagger-like weapon; گزین کرد = 'chose'). پیکان چو آب places پیکان ('arrows') with چو آب ('like water') — a simile meaning 'arrows in a stream like water' i.e. many arrows; چو introduces the comparison.
segments.s668-g112: نهاده is a perfect/past participle 'having placed/put' used as the verb 'he placed/fit'. برو here is an archaic compressed form (originally بر او = 'on it/on them') meaning 'on (their) shafts'; چار is the numeral 'four' modifying پر 'feathers'; عقاب 'eagle' qualifies the feathers: 'four eagle-feathers (were set on them)'.
segments.s668-g369: وزان پس = an emphatic temporal marker 'from that time on / thereafter' (وزان + پس). تو خود is emphatic 'you yourself' (تو + خود). جنگ پرموده is a noun phrase meaning 'well‑practiced / well‑prepared war' (جنگ as head, پرموده as adjectival/participial modifier). ساز here is an imperative/casual causative sense ('make / prepare'), so the whole clause literally says 'from that time on, you yourself make/prepare a well‑practiced battle.' Word order is slightly inverted in poetry: subject/emphatic (تو خود) + object phrase (جنگ پرموده) + verb (ساز as 'make').
segments.s668-g370: ممان is an archaic/jussive sense 'do not withhold / do not desist' (negative/forbidding nuance). تا introduces a subordinate temporal clause 'until'; شود is third‑person singular present/subjunctive 'becomes / comes to be' used here as 'until X happens'; خصم is the patient 'the enemy'; گردن‌فراز is a predicate adjective 'proud, haughty.' Together: 'do not stop until the enemy (who is) proud is subdued' (i.e. until the proud enemy is brought low).
segments.s668-g371: هم at the start gives 'also / too.' ایرانیان را is a fronted/marked recipient (را here marks the definite/affected noun 'the Iranians' as the indirect object). فرستاد is past tense 'he sent / dispatched.' چیز is the (indefinite) thing sent: 'a thing / something' — in context a written document. Poetic word order places the recipient before the verb and the thing after it.
segments.s668-g372: نبشته is a written text/inscription (the message itself). به هر شهر is a prepositional phrase 'to every city' (به + هر + شهر). منشور is an inscribed charter/decree (here a physical or formal proclamation). نیز at the end adds 'also / likewise.' Word order: written‑item + destination + noun (منشور) + adverb (نیز).
segments.s668-g373: فرستاده را uses را to mark the (definite) person affected: 'the envoy.' خلعت is the gift/robe of honour (direct object or oblique item in the action of bestowal). آراستند is past plural 'they adorned / they arrayed' (transitive past). The clause means 'they arrayed the envoy with a robe of honour' — classical Persian often puts the recipient and garment around the verb in this order.
segments.s668-g374: پس is a temporal/connecting 'then.' اسب جهان‌پهلوان is a genitival/grouped noun meaning 'the horse/steed of the world‑champion' (اسب modified by جهان‌پهلوان). خواستند is past plural 'they asked for / desired / requested.' The clause therefore: 'then they requested the champion's horse.'
segments.s668-g375: چون … شد is a classical temporal clause 'when … happened / when … came to be.' فرستاده is subject 'the envoy.' پیش + بهرام means 'in the presence of Bahram / before Bahram.' شد is the past/resultive 'came / arrived / became' used here with the sense 'came before.' So the clause reads 'when the envoy came before Bahram.'
segments.s668-g376: سپهدار is the title 'the commander.' از here is the causal/agentive sense 'because of / on account of (him)' (an older idiom, often contracted in manuscripts), so 'سپهدار از و شاد' = 'the commander rejoiced because of him.' و is simply coordinating 'and.' پدرام is a proper name; شد is past 'became.' So: 'the commander rejoiced at him, and Pedram became glad (also).' Note the archaic sequence 'از و' = 'از او' ('because/for him').
segments.s668-g289: ز here is the poetic/prepositional form used in comparisons (‘than’/‘from’); رستم is the proper name Rostam. فزونست is an archaic/past‑or‑present copular form meaning ‘is/was greater’ (fozon‑st), and هنگام جنگ is a time phrase ‘at the time of battle’. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: literally ‘than Rostam greater-is at the time of battle’ = ‘in battle he was greater than Rostam’.
segments.s668-g290: دلیران is the plural subject ‘the brave/warriors’. نگیرند is the present/optative negative form of گرفتن (here ‘they do not take’), used with the noun درنگ to mean ‘do not take a pause/they do not pause’. پیشش is a locative/pronominal compound ‘before/in the presence of him’. The clause therefore reads ‘the brave do not take a pause before him’.
segments.s668-g291: نبد is an archaic negative existential (‘there was not’ / ‘was not’). لشکرش is ‘his army/host’ (لشکر + possessive -ش) with را as the definite/object marker. ز ما صد یکی literally ‘one of a hundred from us’ = ‘one of ours in a hundred’ — a comparative expression meaning no man in his host equaled even one of a hundred of ours. The phrase order is elliptical and poetic.
segments.s668-g292: نخست ‘first/foremost’ modifies what follows. از introduces the partitive/part reference ‘of/from’, so نخست از دلیران ما = ‘first among our brave’. کودکی here is a nominal meaning ‘a child/a youth’ (used for a young champion). The copula is omitted (typical in poetry), so the sense is ‘the foremost of our brave was a youth’.
segments.s668-g293: جهان‌دار is a title ‘world‑ruler/possessor of the world’; یزدان is the divine name ‘God’. ورا is the clitic form of ‘to him’ (or ‘toward him’). برکشید is a past tense verb ‘raised up’/‘lifted up’. The clause literally reads ‘the world‑ruler God raised him up’.
segments.s668-g294: ازین بیش is a fused demonstrative phrase ‘from this / more than this’ (از + این), i.e. ‘beyond this’. گویم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of گفتن ‘I say’ (here used idiomatically). نباید is the impersonal negative ‘one must not/should not’. شنید is the verbal infinitive/passive sense ‘be heard’/‘be heard of’. The whole construction is poetic: ‘I should not say more than this’ or literally ‘from this more I say — it must not be heard’, i.e. ‘I ought not to be heard saying more.’
segments.s668-g295: چو ... بشنید is a temporal clause: چو = “when.” پرموده is an epithet (an adjective) modifying the implicit subject (the experienced man); بشنید is simple past 3rd‑person “he heard.” گفتار اوی = “his speech,” where اوی is the possessive ’his’ referring back to a previously mentioned person.
segments.s668-g296: Literal order: [full‑of thought] became‑he heart from deed his. پر + اندیشه = “full (of) thought”; گشتش = گشت + ـش, past “became/turned” with 3rd‑person clitic; دل = “heart.” از کار اوی = “concerning/about his deed(s),” where از marks the relation and اوی is the possessive.
segments.s668-g145: چو + past verb (بگذشت) is a temporal clause meaning 'when/after'. زان روز بد is a frozen demonstrative phrase 'that bad day'; به زمان is a poetic locator ('in/through time' or 'as time passed'). The whole segment thus gives the temporal setting: 'when that bad day had passed.'
segments.s668-g146: ندیدند is the past negative verb 'they did not see'. زنده یکی is an indefinite object phrase 'a single living one' (word order: adjective/noun + numeral). بد گمان is an adjectival/participial phrase meaning 'thinking the worst' or 'in ill hope' that modifies their state. Together: 'they did not see even one living person, thinking the worst.'
segments.s668-g147: مگرآنک introduces an exception ('except that/for those who'). بودند is the past plural of 'to be' and گشته is a perfect participle meaning 'become/been made'; اسیر is the predicate noun 'captives'. The clause means 'except those who had become captives.'
segments.s668-g148: روان‌ها (souls/spirits) followed by the prepositional phrase به غم خسته ('worn/tired with grief') describes their inner state; و links to تن به تیر where تن (body) + به تیر ('by/with arrows') states the physical condition. This is a compact, poetic coordination: 'their souls weary with sorrow and their bodies (pierced) by arrows.'
segments.s668-g149: همه راه ('every/whole road') is the subject; برگستوان is an archaic/verbal noun meaning 'turning back/return' (here describing purpose or character of the road) and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause states a general condition: 'all the roads were for retreat/return.'
segments.s668-g150: سران را uses را as a marker to make 'the chiefs' the affected topic. ز ترگ آمده is a poetic sequence: ز ('from/because of') + ترگ ('rag') + آمده (past participle/verb 'has come'); روز مرگ ('the day of death') names what has arrived. Literally: 'for the chiefs, from rags the day of death had come' — i.e. 'with rags on their heads the chiefs faced the day of death.'
segments.s668-g151: A simple noun list: همان ('that/the same') modifies تیغ هندی ('Indian sword'), and the conjunctions link تیر (arrow) and کمان (bow). It's an enumerative phrase naming the weapons involved: 'that very Indian sword and the arrows and the bow.'
segments.s668-g152: به هرسوی ('to every side' / 'everywhere') is a directional prepositional phrase; افگنده is the past participle 'thrown' or 'cast' used predicatively; بد بدگمان is a terse descriptive phrase — بد ('ill/bad') + بدگمان ('suspicious/causing fear') — conveying that the weapons lay scattered about and caused people to dread the worst. The whole segment: 'thrown in every direction, ill-omened / causing dread.'
segments.s668-g57: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. از دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar' (preposition از + noun دور). The phrase is arranged in SOV order typical of Persian: subject + object (بهرام پیلان) precedes the verb بدید, which is 3rd-person singular past 'saw'.
segments.s668-g58: غمی گشت is a light-verb construction: غمی (sorrow) + گشت (became/turned) = 'became sorrowful'. و links two coordinated clauses. تیغ is the direct object and از میان is an adverbial phrase 'out of/from the midst'; برکشید is the 3rd-person singular past meaning 'drew/raised' (here 'drew his sword').
segments.s668-g59: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial meaning 'after that' (از + demonstrative آن + پس). چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = thus; گفت = said). با مهتران is a prepositional complement 'with/to the chiefs' (با = with). گفت is a past verb governing the following quoted address.
segments.s668-g60: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/exclamatory address (equivalent to 'O ...'). نام‌داران و جنگ آوران are two coordinated nouns addressed by the speaker: نام‌داران 'renowned ones' and جنگ‌آوران 'those who bring/engage in battle' (i.e. warriors). آوران is an agentive plural 'bringers' attached to جنگ.
segments.s668-g61: کمانهای is the plural construct 'the bows'. چاچی appears as a modifier of the bows (likely an ethnic or place-name adjective here); because the provided senses do not match that meaning it is marked below as uncertain. بزه is the direct object of برنهید; given the verb, برنهید is an imperative/jussive form ('put on' / 'arm yourselves with') addressing the addressees, so the clause reads 'put on/arm (the) ... bows.'
segments.s668-g62: همه یکسره is an adverbial phrase 'all (of you) straightaway/completely'. ترگ is the thing to be placed (here 'a rag' or cloth) and برسرنهید is again the imperative 'place on the head' (بر سر = on the head + نهید, imperative/optative form). The verb governs ترگ (object) and the prepositional element برسر indicates location.
segments.s668-g63: به‌جان و سر is a fixed oath-forming phrase 'by the life and head' (به‌جان = by one's life; سر = head). شهریار جهان is a genitival phrase 'the king/sovereign of the world' (شهریار = king; جهان = world), with the possessor preceding the possessee as is normal in Persian.
segments.s668-g64: گزین is an imperative form 'choose/select' addressing the addressees. It governs two coordinated objects: بزرگان 'the great ones' (elder/important men) and تاج مهان 'the crown for/of the guests' (تاج = crown; مهان = guests). The verb applies to both coordinated noun phrases.
segments.s668-g281: ازآن پس is a temporal phrase meaning “from then on / after that.” گوان‌را is the direct object plus the object marker -را (the word گوان here is a poetic/oblique word-form supplied by the verse). بر خویش is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/onto himself” (بر + خوی + pronominal ش), and خواند is a past tense verb here meaning “he called/summoned.” Word order is literary: temporal phrase -> object -> goal -> verb.
segments.s668-g282: به‌مژگان is an adverbial/circumstantial phrase “at/with the eyelashes” (a poetic image). همی is the archaic continuous/iterative particle meaning “continually / was (so)”; خون دل is a compound noun “blood of the heart” (a metaphor for deep sorrow/tears). برفشاند is a present/past narrative-form verb here meaning “he scattered/sprinkled/spattered (upon).” The clause describes a repeated, poetic action.
segments.s668-g283: بپرسید is a past-tense narrative verb “he asked.” کز here is the relative/prepositional k- meaning “who/which/of whom,” and لشکر بی‌شمار is the noun phrase “the countless army/host.” Together they form the topic of the question introduced by بپرسید.
segments.s668-g284: که introduces the relative clause “who/that.” در رزم is the locative phrase “in battle.” جستن is the infinitive “to attempt/seek” used here with نکردند (negative past plural) to mean “they did not attempt/perform,” and کار (“deed”) is the object placed after the verb phrase — literal order is “… did not do deed.” The clause therefore says which members of the host failed to perform deeds in battle.
segments.s668-g285: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so.” داد is the past verb “gave.” پاسخ is the direct object “answer,” ورا (ورا = به + را/ورا) is the dative-like clitic meaning “to him,” and رهنمون is a noun meaning “guidance/guide.” Word order is literary but straightforward: “Thus he gave him an answer and guidance.”
segments.s668-g286: که begins the content clause (“that…”). ما داشتیم is past-tense plural “we had” (داشتیم = had). آن سپه را uses the demonstrative + noun + object marker “that host (as definite object),” and زبون is an adjectival/predicative description meaning “brought low / put to abasement” (i.e. defeated). The clause reports the content of the answer.
segments.s668-g287: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when.” بهرام is the proper name. جنگی is an adjective/noun here meaning “warlike / the warrior,” and بهنگام کار is a temporal phrase “at the timely moment for action” (بهنگام = at the time of; کار = action/deed). The whole segment sets the temporal circumstance for what follows.
segments.s668-g288: نبیند is the past/narrative form of 'to see' used in the clause (“he saw” or in a negated construction “saw not”). کس here is used in the negative sense “anyone/no one” (classical Persian often inverts word order around the verb), اندر جهان is the locative “in the world,” and یک سوار is “a single horseman.” The phrasing is literary: the verb precedes کس, yielding the sense “he did not see a single horseman in the world.”
segments.s668-g241: سران‌شان ('their leaders/heads') is the direct object (head + possessive). ببرید is a past/poetic action meaning 'cut off' (here describing the violent act); in classical/poetic Persian the perfective/past form can appear without the later -ند ending. یکسر ('entirely/cleanly') modifies the following prepositional phrase ز تن ('from the body') and emphasizes that the heads were severed completely. Word order is object + verb + adverbial phrase, typical of Persian poetry where modifiers cluster after the verb for effect.
segments.s668-g242: کسی را که is a relative/indefinite clause 'anyone who/whom' (کسی = 'someone'; راکه = را + که, the object/relative marker plus 'who/that'), introducing which persons were affected. بد modifies مهتر as an epithet meaning 'bad/ill' (wicked or ill‑famed chief). انجمن ('assembly' or 'council') completes the noun phrase: 'anyone who was a bad chief of the council.' The relative clause links back to the heads named in the previous segment.
segments.s668-g243: درفشی درفشان is a doubled epithet: درفشی (relating to the banner or banner‑bearer) followed by درفشان ('shining, glittering'), forming a vivid compound epithet 'the glittering standard/bearer.' پس 'then' is a temporal/sequence particle that introduces what follows. هر سری = 'each/every head' (هر = every, سری = head/chief) and is the phrase picked out by the following relative clause. The phrase order places the epithet and temporal particle before the noun phrase for poetic emphasis.
segments.s668-g244: که بودند ... is a relative clause 'which/who were ...' with بودند as the past-tense copula 'they were.' از آن introduces provenance 'from those/among those ...' and افسری is an adjectival/nominal qualifier meaning something like 'helmeted/with diadem' (i.e., bearing a helmet or diadem). Grammatically the clause modifies 'هر سری' to specify which heads are meant: 'every head that among those (fighters) bore a helmet.'
segments.s668-g245: اسیران و سرها همه is a coordinated noun phrase 'the captives and the heads, all of them.' گرد کرد is a common Persian verbal compound where گرد (here verbal/denominal) + کرد 'made/did' = 'gathered/collected' (i.e., 'caused to be round/gathered together'). The subject (implicit in the narrative) is the actor who 'made them be gathered.' Word order places the list-of-objects before the verb phrase, as is normal.
segments.s668-g246: ببردند is a past plural 'they took/carried off' (here 'they carried away' the gathered captives and heads). ز آوردگاه نبرد is a postpositional phrase 'from the field/place of battle' (ز = 'from', آوردگاه = 'battlefield/venue', نبرد = 'battle'), giving the source from which they removed the captives and heads. The verb+source phrase closes the action begun in the previous segment.
segments.s668-g247: دبیر is the subject 'the secretary.' نویسنده را ('the scribe' + object marker را) is the definite object: the secretary 'called' or 'summoned' him. پیش ('forward; before/in the presence of') indicates direction/position — 'called forward' or 'into the presence.' خواند here is used in the sense 'summoned/called' (one of its poetic senses alongside 'read'), so the clause means 'the secretary called the scribe forward.'
segments.s668-g248: ز هر در ('from every gate/quarter') is an idiomatic locative phrase meaning 'from every direction/quarter' (ز = 'from', هر در = 'every gate/quarter'). فراوان modifies سخن‌ها: 'many/much speech(s)' (familiar in Persian as 'many words/utterances'). براند is a verbal form here meaning 'he produced/uttered/drove out' the many words — poetically 'he set many speeches forth.' The verb is used figuratively (driving forth words), hence the driving sense works metaphoricallly.
segments.s668-g193: This clause begins with the prepositional phrase به‌ما (to/for us) marking the person affected, followed by اختر بد ('an unlucky/ill star'). چنین بازگشت uses بازگشت as a past action verb ('returned/turned'), with چنین 'thus' modifying the verb. Word order is typical Persian: affected party (به‌ما) + subject/phrase (اختر بد) + verb (بازگشت).
segments.s668-g194: همان رنج ('that same suffering') is the noun phrase subject. با باد ('by/with the wind') marks the agent/instrument; انباز is an archaic/uncertain word modifying how the action happened, and گشت is the verb 'became/was turned' (here passive/result sense). The clause literally says the suffering was turned/carried off by the wind.
segments.s668-g195: This conditional clause opens with اگر ('if'). یابم is a subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may I obtain / if I obtain' rather than a simple indicative. از تو is the source 'from you', and به جان is an oath formula 'by my life' (به here used adverbially). زینهار functions as a noun 'a warning/admonition' being requested.
segments.s668-g196: This is the expected result clause: یکی ('a certain/one') + پر هنر ('full of skill') is a noun phrase describing the person. یافتی is used in a resultive sense 'you would find/obtain' (classical past/optative flavor), and دست‌وار is a compound/adjective meaning something like 'at hand' or 'ready by hand.' The adjective phrase follows the noun, as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s668-g197: چو ('when') introduces a temporal clause. بشنید is a past tense 'he heard' with بهرام as the subject; و اندیشه کرد is a compound verb phrase where اندیشه 'thought' takes کرد as the light verb — 'he thought/pondered.' The two verbs are coordinated to give the sequence 'when Bahram heard and pondered.'
segments.s668-g198: دلش ('his heart' with the clitic ش = 'his') is the topic; گشت here means 'became' and governs the predicate پر درد ('full of pain'). The conjunction و links the second predicate رخساره زرد ('his countenance pale'), where the copula is omitted but understood from گشت earlier — a common elliptical pattern in Persian.
segments.s668-g199: زمانی همی‌گفت is an iterative past construction: زمانی 'for a while' + همی‌گفت 'would keep saying' (habitual past). کین روز جنگ is a noun phrase 'this/that day of battle' (کین here is an archaic demonstrative/marker in context, not the common sense 'vengeance' — that specific demonstrative sense is not listed among the provided senses).
segments.s668-g200: به کار آیدم is an idiomatic predicate 'be of use to me' (به marks beneficiary/target and آیدم 'I will come / I will be of use'). چو شود کار تنگ is a temporal subordinate clause 'when کار تنگ becomes' — چو 'when', شود 'becomes', کار تنگ 'matters become tight/urgent' (تنگ 'tight/desperate').
segments.s668-g33: چنان prefaces the noun as an intensifier ('such'), followed by the noun لشکری ('army/troops'). رابه‌هم (به‌هم with the accusative marker رـ) marks the result/way of the action — 'into disorder/to pieces.' بردرید is a perfective past verb (poetic form) meaning 'tore apart' and takes the preceding phrase as its object; subject is implied from context.
segments.s668-g34: درفش سپه‌دار is a nominal phrase 'the commander's banner/standard' (poetic omission of an ezafe is common). شد + adjective/participial predicate (ناپدید) expresses change/result: 'became vanished' → 'vanished.' شد here functions as a resultive auxiliary.
segments.s668-g35: و زان جایگه sets the starting point ('and from that place'). شد here indicates motion/change of position ('turned/went') and governs سوی قلب‌گاه ('toward the heart/center'); قلب‌گاه is a figurative locative noun. Word order (starting place → شد → direction) is typical of epic/poetic narrative.
segments.s668-g36: بران سو که = 'to that side where...' — بران + سو = directional phrase. که introduces the relative/subordinate clause describing that side. سالار بد با سپاه is a verbless nominal description 'the evil/wicked commander with (his) army' (the verb 'was/stood' is omitted but implied).
segments.s668-g37: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (indirect object) placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past verb 'he said' (here listed senses did not include the verb sense, so index is -1). برگشت باد is an optative formula: برگشت 'return/turn back' and باد is the optative particle meaning 'may it be'; این سخن = 'this saying/these words' is the object of that wish.
segments.s668-g38: گر‌ای دون combines the conditional particle گر ('if') with the vocative ای ('O'), addressing someone as 'O base one' (دون = 'low/base'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when' (here functioning to lead into the conditional content). این رزم گردد کهن: این رزم 'this battle'; گردد is a present/subjunctive/optative form 'become' (marks potential/hypothetical result in the conditional), and کهن is the predicate adjective 'old/ancient.'
segments.s668-g39: پراکنده گردد is an impersonal/resultative statement: پراکنده 'scattered' + گردد (present/subjunctive often with future/resultive sense) → 'will be scattered' or 'shall be dispersed.' به جنگ این سپاه is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in the battle, this army' (the order places the predicate before the topical noun).
segments.s668-g40: نگه کن is an imperative 'look/watch!' (نگه 'gaze/watch' + کن imperative). کنون 'now' is an adverb. تا here introduces purpose/result ('so that / in order that') leading to the interrogative nominal clause کدامست راه ('which way it is' / 'which road it is'); کدامست is an enclitic interrogative 'which is it.'
segments.s668-g249: از آن is a two-word prepositional phrase here meaning “of/about that” (anaphoric reference to something just mentioned). لشکر is the head noun “army/host”; adjectives follow the noun in Persian, so نامور (renowned) and بی‌شمار (countless) modify لشکر.
segments.s668-g250: Another repeated از آن (“of/about that”) introduces a second topic. جنبش و گردش form a coordinated nominal phrase “movement and rotation/revolution”; روزگار here means the course of time or fate (گردشِ روزگار = the turning of fortune).
segments.s668-g251: از آن again “of/about that.” چاره and جنگ are coordinated nouns (“remedy/plan and war”). واز is the poetic/contracted form of و + از (“and from”); هر modifies دری (here meaning “every quarter/door/way”), so واز هر دری = “and from every quarter.”
segments.s668-g252: کجا is a rhetorical interrogative “where”; رفته is the past participle/verb “gone” and with the adjective بد forms a resultative phrase meaning “has gone badly/has turned out ill.” با چنان لشکری is an adjunct “with such an army” (با = with, چنان = such, لشکری = troops/host).
segments.s668-g253: و زان is “and from that” (زان is an archaic/poetic form of از آن). کوشش و جنگ ایرانیان is a noun phrase meaning “the effort/striving and the Iranians’ war” (ایرانیان is the genitive/possessive group).
segments.s668-g254: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the result or circumstance. نگشاد here functions as a past negative sense ‘did not open/occur’ (classical/poetic form); روزی means “a day” (not the verb senses listed in the lexicon) and سواری is “rider/horseman”; میان = “among/between.” Together the clause says that not even a single day a horseman opened/appeared among them.
segments.s668-g255: چو = “when.” آن نامه بنوشت is an archaic past clause: بنوشت is the poetic/past form related to نوشت (wrote/was written); here it reads “when that letter was written/presented.” نزدیک شاه functions adverbially as “near the king” / “before the king,” indicating the action’s relation to the king.
segments.s668-g256: گزین کرد is a two-word verbal sequence “(he) chose” (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made — standard past-tense construction). گوینده‌ای is the indefinite object “a speaker/messenger”; زان سپاه is a genitive phrase “from that army” (زان = از آن). Word order is Subject (شاه implicit/previous) – Verb – Object.
segments.s668-g41: A sequence of perfective verbs followed by an existential negation: برفتند (third‑person plural past) + وجستند (third‑person plural past, here 'they sprang/jumped') describe the actors' movement; راهی نبود is an existential statement 'there was no way/path' where راهی is an indefinite noun 'a way' and نبود is the past negative of 'to be'.
segments.s668-g42: کزان = 'from which' (کَه + زِ آن), introducing a relative/adverbial phrase that refers back to the absent route; راه is the noun 'path'; شایست is a poetic/truncated form meaning 'fitting/suitable' (shorthand for شایسته) qualifying راه; بالا is an adverb 'upwards' and نمود is the past of نمودن 'show/appear' — together 'a suitable path upward appeared.'
segments.s668-g43: چنین گفت is 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); با introduces an accompaniment 'with' and links to the following noun phrase. لشکر آرای خویش is a poetic compound: لشکر 'army' modified by آرای (here 'array/ornament/arrayed force') and completed by the possessive enclitic ش attached to خویش, meaning 'his own' — together 'with his arrayed army.'
segments.s668-g44: که introduces reported speech or a subordinate clause 'that'; دیوار ما = 'our wall' (ما as possessive here); آهنینست is the adjective 'آهنین' ('iron, iron‑made') with the copula (آهنین + است contracted to آهنینست) meaning 'is iron/ironlike'; پیش here means 'before/in front (of you)' indicating position relative to the addressee.
segments.s668-g45: هر آنکس که = 'whosoever' / 'every one who' (relative clause introducer); او is the emphatic pronoun 'he' (used here in the relative clause); رخنه داند زدن is a light‑verb construction: داند 'knows' plus the infinitive/verb زدن (here in the sense of producing/striking a breach) — together 'who knows how to make a breach.'
segments.s668-g46: ز = 'from' (poetic preposition); دیوار = 'the wall' (object of the preposition); بیرون شدن is the verbal phrase 'to go out / to get outside' (بیرون + شدن); تواند is the modal 'is able' (third‑person), so together ز دیوار بیرون تواند شدن = 'is able to get out from the wall.' Word order places the prepositional phrase up front in poetic style.
segments.s668-g47: شود ایمن = 'he will be safe' or potential 'may become safe' (شود is the classical present/future/optative form of شدن + ایمن 'safe'); و جان به ایران برد coordinates a second result clause: جان 'life' is the object and به ایران برد is 'take/carry to Iran' (برد here is the verb 'to take/carry' rather than the noun 'victory'), so 'and carry his life back to Iran.'
