segments.s668-g48: به نزدیک = 'to the vicinity of / near'; شاه دلیران is a poetic apposition/epithet: شاه 'the king' qualified by دلیران, here functioning as an epithet 'of the brave' or 'the brave king'; برد is the same verb as above ('carried/brought'), so the clause says '(he) brought (it) near the brave king' — the subject is omitted but continues from the previous clause.
segments.s668-g89: This is a short clause: 'یکی' = an indefinite 'someone'; 'تخت زرین' is a noun phrase 'golden throne'; 'نهاده' is the past participle/verb 'placed/has placed' (classical past form of نهاد used poetically). 'بروی' here functions as 'بر روی' = 'upon/on it' (a prepositional phrase), but that prepositional meaning is not represented in the supplied verb senses, so it is marked separately below.
segments.s668-g90: 'نشسته' is a past participle 'seated' used predicatively (the copula is omitted in poetic style): 'seated (was) ...'. The following 'برو' is another spelling/contraction of 'بر او/بر روی' = 'on it' and functions with 'نشسته' to mean 'seated on it' (this spatial sense is not among the supplied senses, so given as -1). 'ساوه رزم‌جوی' is an inverted genitive: 'رزم‌جوی' (warrior) modified by the place-name 'ساوه' = 'the warrior of Saveh' (poetic word order puts possessor after the head noun).
segments.s668-g91: 'سپه' (the army/host) is the subject and 'دید' is the simple past 'saw' (S + past verb). 'چون' introduces a simile 'like/as'; 'کوه آهن' is a poetic noun phrase 'a mountain of iron' (آهن used image-wise for metal/arms); 'روان' after that describes the state of the mass — 'in motion' or 'moving' (adjectival/participial use).
segments.s668-g92: 'همه' quantifies 'all/every'; 'سر' is 'head' (here 'every head'); 'پر از' is the fixed phrase 'full of' followed by the substance 'گرد' = 'dust'. The conjunction 'و' links the second descriptive phrase 'تیره روان' where 'تیره' = 'dark/gloomy' and 'روان' = 'spirit/soul' (so 'with gloomy spirits').
segments.s668-g93: 'پس پشت آن' = 'just behind that' (پس used with پشت to mean 'at the rear of'); 'زنده پیلان' literally 'living elephants' (emphatic: real, not dead or wooden); 'مست' is figurative 'drunk/intoxicated' = carried away/excited — describing the elephants' furious state.
segments.s668-g94: 'همی‌کوفتند' is the iterative past 'they kept striking/they were striking' (همی- marks continuous or repeated action). 'آن سپه را' is the definite direct object ('that host' marked by را). 'بدست' here is instrumental/means 'by/with' (they struck the host by/with [their force/tusks/hands]).
segments.s668-g95: 'پر از آب شد' is a verb phrase 'became full of water' = 'filled (with tears)'; 'دیده ساوه شاه' is the possessed noun plus possessor in poetic order: 'the eye of the king of Saveh' (دیده = eye, followed by the possessor 'ساوه شاه'). The copular/auxiliary 'شد' carries the past change of state.
segments.s668-g96: 'بدان' here is the fused form 'ب' (prefix) + 'دان' (to know) = 'to know' or 'that he may know'; 'تا' introduces purpose/result 'so that/in order that'; 'چرا شد' = 'why (it) happened' with شد 'happened'; 'هزیمت سپاه' = 'the rout of the army' (هزیمت = rout, سپاه = army). Altogether: '... so that he might know why the army's rout happened.'
segments.s668-g329: همی‌بود is the archaic progressive prefix همی- + بود (past of بودن), here meaning 'kept/continued to stand.' بر پیش is a prepositional phrase ('toward/in the presence of') with بر + پیش, and به‌پای is another prepositional phrase meaning 'at the (his/their) feet' (poetic contracted form). Together they form a locative: 'kept standing before the gods, at their feet.'
segments.s668-g330: همی‌گفت is again the progressive همی- + گفت (kept saying). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; داور رهنمای is a vocative compound — داور ('divine judge' here) followed by رهنمای ('guide'), so the speaker is directly addressing God as 'O Judge, (be) Guide.'
segments.s668-g331: This clause addresses 'you' with a relative/qualifying participle: بد اندیش ('ill‑thinking' or 'one who thought ill') qualifies the addressee. ما را is the object with را marking 'us,' and تو کردی تباه places the past verb کردی ('you did / you made') after the object — 'you made us ruined.' The order is poetic but follows Persian object + verb patterns.
segments.s668-g332: تویی is the emphatic copula 'thou art' (present). آفریننده is a nominative noun 'creator'; the phrase 'تویی آفریننده هور و ماه' is a predicative statement 'you are the creator of هور and ماه.' هور (here 'light/splendor', poetically standing for the sun) is linked with و ماه ('and the moon').
segments.s668-g333: چنان is an intensifier 'so/thus.' زار و نومید are coordinated adjectives 'wretched and hopeless.' بودم is the past first‑person 'I was.' ز + بخت ('because of fate') gives the cause: 'I was so miserable because of fate.'
segments.s668-g334: که introduces the result clause 'that ...'. دشمن is the subject 'the enemy.' نگون is an archaic adjective meaning 'ill‑fated/bringing down (misfortune)' that characterizes the enemy here. اندر آمد is the older form of آمد (to come/arrive) with اندر (in/into) as a prefixed locative; ز تخت ('from the throne') uses poetic ز='from' — together implying the enemy came to (or from) the throne in the consequential sense (i.e. seized or displaced the throne).
segments.s668-g335: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general). نکرد is the past negative form 'did not make/do.' The following nominal phrase 'این ... سپاه' ('this ... army/host') is the object/complement: جنگی سپاه = 'a warlike army' or 'a host for war.' Poetic word order places the negated verb before this noun phrase: literally '[The] spahbed did not make this a war‑host.'
segments.s668-g336: که introduces the reason/explanation 'for.' یزدان بد here means 'God (was) adverse/ill‑disposed' — بد qualifies the deity's disposition toward the event. این جنگ را ('this battle' with را marking the object/topic) نیک‌خواه ('well‑wisher' used predicatively, i.e. 'did not favor') expresses that God was not favorable to this fight. In modern English: 'for God did not favor this battle.'
segments.s668-g161: به هرجای is a prepositional phrase 'to/into every place' placed before the subject; خراد here is an adjective 'wise' (an epithet) modifying the following proper name برزین, so خراد برزین together = 'the wise Barzin'. بگشت is an archaic past form (poetic contraction of برگشت) meaning 'turned/returned'. The word order (prep phrase + epithet + name + verb) is normal in poetry.
segments.s668-g162: به هر پرده و خیمه‌ای is another prepositional phrase 'to/into every curtain and tent'; هر is the distributive 'every/each' applying to both پرده and خیمه. خیمه‌ای uses the -ای ending to mark an indefinite/individual tent in poetic diction. برگذشت is a past verb with the prefix بر- meaning 'passed by/through', so the clause means 'he passed by every curtain and tent'.
segments.s668-g163: کم آمد زلشکر is an idiomatic/poetic phrasing: کم آمد literally 'came (in) small number' here introducing 'one (only) came forth' from the army; زلشکر is a contracted poetic spelling of زِ لشکر 'from the army' (ز + لشکر). یکی نامور is the noun phrase 'one (a certain) renowned person' that is the thing that 'came'.
segments.s668-g164: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a descriptive clause about that 'one'. بهرام is the proper name; بدنام is an adjective 'notorious/ill‑famed'; آن here is the demonstrative/article 'the/that' pointing to the epithet, and پرهنر is an adjectival epithet 'full of skill' (i.e. skilled). The whole clause apposes and describes the named man.
segments.s668-g165: ز تخم سیاوش is the genealogical phrase 'from the seed/offspring of Siyâvash' (تخم used metaphorically for lineage). گوی here is an archaic/verbal noun sense 'called/said' (used as 'named' or 'so called') and مهتری is a title/epithet ('preeminent'/'chief'); together they form an appositive name or epithet: 'called Mahtri' or 'the preeminent one'.
segments.s668-g166: This is a string of titles/appellatives in apposition: سپهبد is the office/title 'spahbed' (commander), سواری is 'a rider/horseman' (here indicating mounted service), دلاور is the adjective/noun 'valiant/heroic', and سری is an epithet 'head/chief' (used as a high‑rank epithet). The nouns are listed without a copula, a common poetic device to pile up epithets.
segments.s668-g167: همی‌رفت is the imperfective/past‑progressive poetic form 'kept going' (همی‑ prefix signals continuous action); جوینده is the agent noun 'the seeker/searcher'. چون is a simile particle 'as/like', and بیهشان is an old-form 'بیه' + pronoun 'شان' meaning 'without them' or 'deprived of them' — together 'as if without them' describing the manner of his going.
segments.s668-g168: مگر introduces an exception/conditional 'unless'. ز (here spelled separately) is the poetic preposition 'from'; the following و is the old orthographic form of the pronoun 'او' (him), so زو = 'from him'. بیابد is the present/subjunctive form of یافتن 'to find' used for a potential/conditional action ('may find'/'should find'). بجایی نشان = 'a sign to/a pointing toward a place' (نشان = sign, بجایی = 'to/for a place' used adverbially).
segments.s668-g9: خروشید is a past, 3rd-person singular verb in poetic diction meaning “roared/cried out.” The subject (بهرام) follows the verb, a common poetic inversion. کای here is the vocative particle “O,” addressing listeners, and مهتران is a vocative plural “chiefs/elders” (addressed by Bahram). Together this segment is Bahram shouting to the chiefs.
segments.s668-g10: This is a vocative noun phrase that completes the address: بزرگان ایران means “the great ones of Iran,” with the genitive/possessive relation implicit in juxtaposition (grand ones + Iran). و simply links a second group, and کنداوران names that other group/people. The whole phrase names the addressees of the cry.
segments.s668-g11: بدین جادویها is a prepositional phrase ‘by/with these magics’ (بدین = ب + این, “by/with these”), followed by جادویها (the plural ‘sorceries/magics’). مدارید چشم is an exhortation addressed to multiple addressees: مدارید here functions as a negative/bid to not be swayed (poetic imperative sense: ‘do not turn’/‘do not let be moved’), and چشم is the object ‘your gaze/eyes.’ The whole segment means “do not let your eyes be diverted by these spells.”
segments.s668-g12: به جنگ اندر آیید is a plural imperative: به + جنگ + اندر (‘into the battle’) followed by آیید (آییدن, “come/enter”), so ‘enter the battle’ or ‘come into the fight.’ یکسر is an adverb ‘entirely/at once’ modifying the following verb. بخشم is a 1st-person verb from بخشیدن; in classical Persian this present/future-form expresses promise/intention ‘I will give/bestow.’ The object of بخشیدن is omitted and understood from context (e.g. pardon, help, reward).
segments.s668-g13: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause (‘for/because’). آن is the demonstrative ‘that.’ سر به سر is an idiomatic intensifier here meaning ‘head-to-head’ → ‘completely/altogether’ (the two سر’s form the idiom). تنبل is an adjectival predicate ‘lazy/sluggish,’ and وجادویست is a clipped poetic construction meaning ‘and is sorcerous/filled with magic’ (و + جادویست where جادویست = ‘is magical/sorcerous’). Grammatically this clause explains why the previous exhortation was given.
segments.s668-g14: ز چاره برایشان بباید گریست is a sententia of lament: ز (poetic ‘from/because of’) + چاره ‘remedy/means’ gives ‘from lack of remedy’; برایشان = برای + شان ‘for them’; بباید is the impersonal/modal ‘one must/it is necessary’ (classical present of بایستن), and گریست here is the verbal infinitive/masdar form used after بباید to mean ‘to weep/lament.’ The clause thus reads ‘one must weep for them because there is no remedy.’
segments.s668-g15: خروشی (a noun ‘a shout/roar’) is the subject, followed by the verb برآمد ‘arose/issued forth,’ and ز ایرانیان indicates source ‘from the Iranians.’ The noun–verb order (noun then verb) is natural here: ‘a shout arose from the Iranians.’
segments.s668-g16: ببستند is a 3rd‑person plural perfect/past verb ‘they closed/put an end to.’ خون ریختن is a verbal noun phrase ‘the spilling/shedding of blood,’ followed by the object marker را identifying that phrase as the direct object. میان (‘among/between’) indicates the sphere in which the action took place. Altogether: ‘they put an end to blood-shedding among (themselves/the ranks).’
segments.s668-g209: A poetic nominal enumeration: three abstract nouns joined by و (and). The nouns are simply listed one after another (word order is noun – و – noun), functioning as a series of qualities being attributed; there is no finite verb in this segment because the list continues into the next segment.
segments.s668-g210: Continues the enumeration. بلندی is a nominal quality (’height/loftiness’) joined by و to a genitive-like phrase نیروی شاهنشهی where نیروی (the noun 'strength/power') is linked to شاهنشهی (’imperial/kingly rule’) as its domain; the wording is compact/poetic (no ezāfe particle written) so read as 'the loftiness and the power of kingship.'
segments.s668-g211: Two nouns joined by و (نژندی and شادمانی) with هم 'also' emphasising the second; ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and it attaches to the following element to mark origin or source. Here the phrase functions as a predicate/clause meaning 'Disgrace and also rejoicing are from you' (تست in this line functions archaically as a second-person reference — see note).
segments.s668-g212: A nominal phrase with a relative clause: انوشه دلیری = 'Anoushe's bravery' (name + quality), followed by که introducing a relative clause modifying دلیری. راه توجست is a compact, poetic expression: توجست (here a noun-form meaning 'dread/apprehension') relates to راه, so the relative clause says that this bravery produced fear in the road/way (i.e. the way itself was made fearful).
segments.s668-g213: A short narrative clause: و زان پس = 'and after that' (زان پس = thereafter), بیامد is the past tense verb 'came', and دبیر بزرگ is the (inverted/poetic) subject 'the great scribe'. Word order here puts the temporal phrase first, the verb next, and the subject after the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s668-g214: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said' (چنین = thus, گفت = said). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee; پهلوان سترگ is the addressee 'mighty hero' (poetic epithet). The whole is an introduction to direct speech: 'thus he said, O mighty champion...'.
segments.s668-g215: An appositive identification followed by a simile: فریدون یل = 'Fereydun, (the) hero' (name + appositive noun). چون تویک پهلوان = 'a champion such as you' — چون 'like', تویک (poetic contraction of 'تو یک') = 'you alone/you one', precedes پهلوان 'champion', a compact poetic order meaning 'a champion like you'.
segments.s668-g216: ندید is the past negative 'did not see'; و نه coordinates the negation ('and not' → 'nor'). کسری نوشین روان is a compact epithet: کسری (Kisra/Khosrow) modified by نوشین 'sweet/delightful' and روان 'soul/spirit' (no ezāfe explicitly written in the poetry), yielding 'a Khosrow of sweet/pleasant spirit.'
segments.s668-g217: The phrase places the object 'همت شیرمردی' (the resolve/valor of a brave man) before the verb; 'هم' is an enclitic 'also/too' adding emphasis. 'اورند' here is a poetic/archaic form corresponding to 'آورند' (they bring/they brought), and 'و بند' appends 'and a bond/fetter' as an additional object. The overall structure is [object] + [verb] + و + [additional object].
segments.s668-g218: 'که' introduces a purposive/wish clause ('so that' / 'that'), followed by the negative optative 'مبادا' (may it not be). 'هرگز' strengthens the negation ('never'), 'به جان‌ت' is a dative phrase 'to/onto your life', and 'گزند' is the nominal predicate 'harm' — together: 'that never may harm befall your life.' Word order places the negator before the noun phrase (مبادا گزند).
segments.s668-g219: Subject phrase 'همه شهر ایران' ('all the cities/towns of Iran') precedes the prepositional dative 'به تو' ('because of/thanks to you' or 'to you'), with the verb 'زنده‌اند' (they are alive) in 3rd-person plural. 'زنده‌اند' is a contracted present of 'زنده هستند'.
segments.s668-g220: The subject 'همه پهلوانان' ('all the champions') is followed by the emphasized/definite object 'تو را' (literally 'you' with the object marker 'را'), and the predicate 'بنده‌اند' ('they are servants/devotees') in 3rd-person plural. The construction means 'all champions are (devoted) to you.'
segments.s668-g221: 'بتو' (to you) is a dative clitic introducing the beneficiary; 'گشت' is the past/perfect of 'گشتن' here meaning 'became/came to be'. The noun phrase 'بخت بزرگی بلند' ('a great, lofty fortune') is the subject/predicate of that verbal action — classical inverted order places the dative first for emphasis.
segments.s668-g222: 'به‌تو' again marks the beneficiary/cause ('because of you' / 'to you'), 'زیردستان' is the plural subject 'your subordinates/subjects', 'شوند' is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive of 'شدن' meaning 'become', and 'ارجمند' is the predicate adjective 'esteemed/valued' — 'your subordinates become esteemed (on your account).' The verb 'شوند' is the standard 3pl auxiliary/predicative form.
segments.s668-g223: 'سپهبد تویی' is a straightforward copular clause: 'you are a spahbed' (تویی = 'thou art'). 'هم سپهبدنژاد' adds 'also/of spahbed-stock' — 'هم' = 'also/too', and 'سپهبدنژاد' is a compound adjective or nisba meaning 'of spahbed lineage'. Word order is noun + copula, then an appositive adjective phrase.
segments.s668-g224: 'خنک مام' is an epithet ('a pleasant/cool mother'), 'کو' is an archaic/interrogative particle meaning 'where/which is' (here 'which/what... is there'), 'چون تو فرزند' ('a child like you') is the object phrase, and 'زاد' is the classical past of 'زادیدن' meaning 'bore/gave birth'. The line asks rhetorically which mother produced a son like you; word order follows older poetic inversion.
segments.s668-g169: تن is the subject 'body'. The two adjectives خسته و کشته follow it in sequence ('weary and slain / battle-worn'); چندی is an adverb meaning 'for a while'; کشید is the simple past of کشیدن here meaning 'to endure/suffer/drag on'. The verb comes at the end as typical in Persian poetry and there is an implied subject 'he/it' referring to the man whose body is described.
segments.s668-g170: ز is the (poetic) preposition 'from/of'; بهرام is the name 'Bahram' and is the source/center of the negated perception; جایی here means 'anywhere / any trace of a place' (i.e. 'whereabouts'); نشانی is 'sign/trace'; ندید is the past negative 'did not see'. The object phrase precedes the verb as normal and the negation is on the verb.
segments.s668-g171: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander'. زان (ز + ان) means 'from that / because of that' and links the following noun کار 'affair/deed' to the cause; شد is the past of شدن 'became' and takes the predicative adjective دردمند 'sorrowful/in pain'. Word order places the causal phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s668-g172: همی‌گفت is the frequentative/past habitual 'kept saying' (he repeatedly said). زار‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory address 'O wretched one' (زار + ای). گو is the imperative or vocative-like particle 'speak/say', and مستمند is another vocative 'needy / afflicted man'. Together they form the command/plea the commander utters.
segments.s668-g173: زمانی is an adverbial 'at that time / then'. برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/appeared' and پدید آمد is a synonymous, slightly more emphatic verb phrase 'appeared'; اوی is the enclitic third‑person pronoun 'he'. The repetition of two verbs is a poetic intensification: 'he arose and appeared'.
segments.s668-g174: در بسته is a noun phrase 'the door being shut / the shut door'; را is the definite/accusative marker attaching to that phrase. چون is a simile particle 'like/as', کلید is the simile's noun 'a key', آمد is the past 'came', and اوی 'he' is the third‑person enclitic subject. The clause means he arrived as if he were the key to the closed door.
segments.s668-g175: ابا here functions as a proper name/identifier ('Aba'); سرخ ترکی is a descriptive epithet 'red Turk' (سرخ modifying ترکی). بد is an attributive adjective 'bad/evil', او is the pronoun 'he', and گربه چشم is a compound adjective 'cat‑eyed' (literally 'cat‑eye'). The sequence lists identifying and pejorative descriptors of the man.
segments.s668-g176: تو گفتی is 'you said' (introducing reported speech). دل‌آزرده دارد is a present construction 'he has a wounded/saddened heart' (دارد + nominal predicate). بخشم here is first‑person singular present/future 'I will forgive / I pardon', expressing the speaker's response; the sequence compresses reported words and the speaker's resolution.
segments.s668-g265: This is a purpose/infinitival clause: ب + دان (بدان) functions like 'to know' or 'in order to learn'; تا introduces the content/purpose ('so that' / 'in order that'); چه is the interrogative 'what' and فرمان دهد is a subordinate clause 'the king may give a command' — دهد is present/subjunctive/optative here ('may give' / 'would give') governed by the purpose particle تا. شهریار is the subject of دهد ('the king').
segments.s668-g266: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent' (third person). با introduces the accompanying instrument/means ('with'); سر فراوان is a quantitative expression 'many heads' i.e. 'a great number' (سر here = 'heads'/'count of people'); سوار is the noun 'horseman' or 'mounted warrior'. The whole phrase attaches as the action of the preceding purpose clause: he sent, with a great number of horsemen.
segments.s668-g267: همان here means 'that same / at the same time'; تا is functioning as a temporal/connecting particle (classical usage) and بود is the past copula 'was'; نیز 'also' emphasizes that this is likewise the case; دستور شاه is the noun phrase 'the king's command' (دستور governed by بود: 'the king's command was ...').
segments.s668-g268: سوی جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'toward war / toward the battlefield'; پرموده is a past‑participle/adjective 'well‑trained' or 'seasoned' describing سپاه; بردن is the infinitive 'to take/lead'; سپاه is the object 'army'. Grammatically this is the complement of دستور شاه بود: 'the king's order was to lead the well‑trained army toward battle.'
segments.s668-g269: ستور نوند is a compound/name (a steed called Navand) where ستور = 'steed/horse' and نوند is a proper name/rare term; اندر آمد ز جای is a past action 'came forth / came out from the place' (اندر آمد = 'came in/forward', ز جای = 'from its place').
segments.s668-g270: به‌پیش سواران is a locative/positional phrase 'before/ahead of the horsemen'; یکی رهنمای = 'one guide' (یکی = 'one / a certain', رهنمای = 'guide'). Word order places the prepositional phrase first and then names the noun (guide) that takes the forward position.
segments.s668-g271: وزان ('and/from that') + روی here functions idiomatically to mark consequence or occasion ('thereupon' / 'on that account'); ترکان همه = 'all the Turks' and برهنه = 'naked, bare' (predicate describing the Turks). Together: 'thereupon all the Turks were naked / went forth naked.'
segments.s668-g272: برفتند is past 'they departed / went off'. بی‌ساز ... بنه is a terse list of what they lacked: بی‌ساز literally 'without equipment/instrument' used here to mean 'without harness or proper gear'; واسب is 'horse/steed' and بنه is 'a bundle / pack' (baggage). The phrase as a whole says they set out naked and without saddles, harness or baggage.
segments.s668-g297: بجوشید is a past narrative of جوشیدن used here figuratively: 'he boiled' (i.e. seethed with feeling). The verb appears before the rest (poetic inversion). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and/then'. رخسارگان is the plural of رخسار (countenances/cheeks). کرد زرد is a light-verb construction where کرد ('made') takes the adjective زرد ('pale') as complement: 'made (his) cheeks pale'.
segments.s668-g298: به‌درد دل is a prepositional/relational phrase 'to/for the pain of the heart' (i.e. concerning his sorrow). آهنگ آورد is a noun + verb collocation: آهنگ 'intention/aim' + آورد 'brought' => 'he set his intention' or 'he resolved'. The final کرد is an auxiliary/light verb reinforcing the action ('made/caused' — a common emphatic or periphrastic pattern in classical Persian: 'آهنگ آورد کرد' = 'he resolved').
segments.s668-g299: سپه is 'army/host' as the topical noun. بودش is بود (past 'was') plus the enclitic -ش indicating 'to him' or 'he had' — together giving 'he had / there was to him'. از before جنگیان is partitive/genitive 'of' (introducing what the army consisted of). Note that the numeral صدهزار follows the noun phrase (classical poetic word order): 'an army of warriors — one hundred thousand.'
segments.s668-g300: همه 'all/everyone' modifies the following adjective نامدار 'renowned'. از در کارزار is a locative/relational phrase 'in the field of battle' (از before در is part of the classical phrasing meaning 'with regard to/in the arena of'); کارزار is the battlefield. Altogether: 'all were renowned in battle.'
segments.s668-g301: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. خرگاه لشکر literally 'the army's خرگاه' — خرگاه here denotes the camp/low quarters (a humble or stable-place for the troops). به‌هامون 'to the Hamun' names the plain/region. کشید is the past of کشیدن 'to draw/move' used here to mean 'they moved/drew (toward)'. Word order places source phrase before the verb, as is common in classical verse.
segments.s668-g302: به نزدیکی is an adverbial phrase 'to the vicinity/near (to)'. رود جیحون is the noun + proper name 'the river Jeyhun' (postnominal name). کشید again is the past verb 'moved/drew' describing the army's advance toward the river.
segments.s668-g303: وزان پس is a temporalconnector: وزان ('from that / and from that') + پس ('after/then') = 'after that' or 'from that time on'. کجا is the interrogative 'whence/where from' placed before the phrase it questions. نامه پهلوان is a genitival phrase 'the hero's letter' (نامه modified by پهلوان). The whole segment forms the question-word fronting typical in classical Persian.
segments.s668-g304: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd person). بر is the dative/prepositional 'to/toward' marking the indirect object that follows. شاه روشن‌روان is the addressee in apposition: 'the king, the bright‑souled' (روشن‌روان is an epithet appended to شاه). The whole segment means 'came to the king, the bright‑souled.'
segments.s668-g377: غنیمت (object) + ببخشید (past verb) form a verb phrase ‘gave/distributed the spoils’. پس is an adverb placed after the verb meaning ‘then’. بر سپاه is a prepositional phrase ‘to/for the army’ marking the beneficiary. The subject is omitted (classical Persian often drops the pronoun).
segments.s668-g378: جز از introduces an exception ‘except from/for’. گنج ناپاک is a noun + adjective ‘unclean/illicit treasure’. دل here is a possessed/nominal element (‘the heart’ or ‘object of feeling’) linked to ساوه شاه as its possessor: دلِ ساوهِ شاه = ‘the heart of the king of Sāveh’ (a compact genitive typical of the poem). The whole phrase therefore means ‘except for the unclean treasure (connected with) the heart of the king of Sāveh.’
segments.s668-g379: فرستاد is the simple past ‘he sent’. تا introduces a purpose clause here (‘so that / in order that’), not a temporal ‘until’. استواران خویش = ‘his steadfast/loyal men’; خویش is the possessive formed in two pieces in the scansion (خوی + ش).
segments.s668-g380: A coordinated noun phrase: جهان‌دیده ‘worldly / well‑traveled / experienced’ and ونام‌داران ‘and the famed / notable ones’, followed by the possessive خویش ‘his’. The conjunction is incorporated into the first token (ونام‌داران). Together they name which men were sent.
segments.s668-g381: ببردند is past 3rd‑plural ‘they took / they carried’. یک‌سر is an adverb meaning ‘at once / straightaway / entirely’ modifying the action. به درگاه شاه is the destination phrase ‘to the royal court (presence) of the king’.
segments.s668-g382: سپهبد is the subject/title ‘the spahbed (general)’. سوی جنگ is a prepositional phrase ‘toward war / to battle’. شد is the past of شدن used here in the sense ‘set out / went / turned (to)’ and با سپاه marks accompaniment ‘with the army’ — together: ‘the spahbed set out for battle with the army.’
segments.s668-g383: ازو is a source/agental prepositional form ‘from him / about him’. چون here functions temporally/causally ‘when / as’. بپرموده شد is a passive/perfect participial construction ‘was instructed/informed’ (بپرموده = instructed; شد = became/was as auxiliary). آگهی is the noun ‘news / information’ and is the piece of information received. Word order is compact/poetic: ‘When from him there was the information (that…)’.
segments.s668-g384: که introduces the subordinate content clause ‘that …’. جوید is the present stem 3rd‑singular of جویدن ‘he seeks / desires’. همی is the classical continuous/progressive marker (‘continually / is in the act of’). تخت شاهنشهی is a compound ‘the throne of kingship / the imperial throne’. Together: ‘that he is seeking the imperial throne.’
segments.s668-g113: بمالید is a past tense verb meaning 'rubbed' (here sense 0). The phrase has the typical Persian SOV grouping where the verb precedes the rest for poetic compression: 'چاچی' (the nurse) is the agent, 'کمان را' is the definite object marked by را, and 'بدست' here means 'in the hand' (not the ablative). So: 'The nurse rubbed the bow in her hand.'
segments.s668-g114: This clause continues with به + noun phrase and a verb: 'به' here means 'into/towards' (sense 1), 'چرم گوزن' = 'deerskin', 'اندر آورد' is a compound-like sequence meaning 'put/placed into' (آورد = bring/put, sense 0), and 'شست' is the noun 'thumb' (sense 1). Grammatically it says she put her thumb into a deerskin (i.e. slipped on a deerskin thumb-glove).
segments.s668-g115: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). 'چپ راست کرد' compresses 'moved/turned left and right' with کرد = 'made/did' (sense 0). The coordination 'و خم آورد راست' continues: خم 'bend/curve' (sense 0) and آورد 'brought/drew' (sense 0); the final 'راست' here means 'to the right' (sense 2). Word order is compact/poetic: actions are juxtaposed under چو.
segments.s668-g116: This is a short clause: 'خروش' (roar/cry) + 'از خم چرخ' (a prepositional phrase 'from the curve/bend of the wheel' — چرخ given the poetic setting is taken as 'wheel' here, sense 1) and then 'چاچی بخاست' where چاچی is the subject and بخاست is the past 'stood up/rose' (sense 0). Note the inversion/conciseness: the phrase describing the source of the cry precedes the verb and the agent 'چاچی' follows, a common poetic word order.
segments.s668-g117: چو 'when' introduces the clause. آورد is the past 'brought/put' (sense 0). 'یال یلی' is a genitive/genitival sequence 'the mane of the hero/warrior' (یال = mane, sense 0; یلی = 'valiant/heroic', sense 0). 'رابه‌گوش' is fused prepositional/adverbial 'to the ear' or 'to his ear' (sense 0). The verb governs the object phrase in compact, poetic order.
segments.s668-g118: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (sense 0). 'شاخ گوزنان' = 'the antlers/horns of the stags' (شاخ horn, sense 0; گوزنان plural of 'deer', sense 0). برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth' (sense 0) and خروش is 'a cry' (sense 0). Note the verb برآمد precedes the noun خروش here — a common poetic inversion where the verbal action comes before the nominal.
segments.s668-g119: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. بگذشت is the past of گذشت meaning 'passed by/went past' (sense 1). 'پیکان' is 'arrow' (sense 0). 'از انگشت اوی' is a prepositional phrase 'past/through his finger' where اوی is the pronominal enclitic meaning 'his' (sense 1). The verb governs the prepositional complement in expected Persian order.
segments.s668-g120: گذر کرد is a verbal periphrasis 'passed/did pass' (گذر here treated as the noun/lexeme for 'passage', sense 0, combined with کرد 'did', sense 0). 'از مهره پشت اوی' is the path/through-phrase: 'through the vertebra of his back' — مهره is used in the anatomical sense 'vertebra' (sense 2), پشت 'back' (sense 0), and اوی is the possessive 'his' (sense 1). The whole clause states the arrow's course through his spinal vertebra.
segments.s668-g185: هران کس که = "هر آن کس که" (anyone who): the compound functions as a relative phrase meaning "whoever." که introduces the relative clause. سالار باشد is a classical present/subjunctive form meaning "is / may be" (used like a relative predicate). به جنگ is a prepositional phrase "in battle/war" modifying "سالار." Word order places the emphasized noun phrase "هران کس" before the verb, typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s668-g186: به کارآیمش = "be (of) use to him": کارآیمش is a verbal form with the enclitic -ش = "him," meaning "be useful to him/avail him." چون بود کارتنگ links a temporal/conditional clause: چون = "when/if/when it was," بود = past copula "was," and کارتنگ (kār-tang) is a nominal compound "narrow/tight affairs." Together: "I will help him when his affairs are tight."
segments.s668-g187: به شب = "at night" (time adverbial). چیزهایی = an indefinite plural "some things" or "certain things." نمایم is 1st person present/past-subjunctive in classical style ("I show / I cause to appear"); بخواب here is an adverbial/locative compound meaning "in sleep / in a dream" (ب + خواب), so the clause means "I show some things to (someone) in sleep at night."
segments.s668-g188: که introduces a purpose/result clause ("so that"). آهستگان را (آهستگان = "the slow/slow-moving ones") is marked by را as the object/topic affected. کنم is 1st person subjunctive/present ("I make / cause"), and پرشتاب is a predicative/adverbial complement meaning "hasty/rapidly." The clause thus means "so that I make the slow ones hurry."
segments.s668-g189: تو را is the fronted, emphasized direct object ("you" with را). من is the subject pronoun; نمودم is the simple past 1st-person "I showed / I made appear." شب آن خواب بد is a temporal + demonstrative + noun phrase "that bad dream at night / in the night." Word order (object first, then subject and verb, then time and object details) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s668-g190: بدان گونه = "in that manner / thus" (بدان is a fused demonstrative/locution meaning 'to/that' + گونه "manner"). تا introduces a purpose/result clause "so that" and governs the following clause. بر سرت = "upon your head / upon you" (بر = on/upon, سرت = your head/you), بد رسد uses رسد (present/subjunctive/classical sense) meaning "may come / befall" and بد = "evil/misfortune." The whole clause means "so that misfortune might befall you."
segments.s668-g191: مرا چاره زان بیش بایست جست = "(for) me a remedy beyond that must be sought": مرا is the object/emphatic construction ("for me" or "as for me"), چاره = "remedy/solution," زان بیش = "more than that / beyond that," and بایست + جست is a classical modal periphrasis: بایست (must/ought) + infinitive جست (to seek) = "must be sought / one must seek." Word order places the logical object/topic early for emphasis.
segments.s668-g192: چو = "since / when" introducing a causal clause. نیرنگ‌ها را is the plural object "the stratagems/deceptions" marked with را. نکردم is the 1st-person past negative "I did not do / I did not employ." درست is an adverb/adjectival complement meaning "properly / correctly." Together: "since I did not employ stratagems properly."
segments.s668-g81: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' followed by the past verb بدید ('saw'). پیل is the subject (the elephant). آن‌چنان modifies the noun phrase زخم پیکان ('wound of arrows') and expresses degree ('so / to such an extent'). Word order is classical: conjunction + subject + degree + noun (with زخم governed by پیکان = 'arrow‑wound').
segments.s668-g82: همه لشکر خویش را is a quantified definite object: همه ('all') + لشکر ('army') + خویش (his own; formed here from خوی + enclitic ش) and the object marker را. بسپرید is a past/preterite lexical verb meaning 'entrusted/handed over' with an implicit subject (he/the elephant).
segments.s668-g83: سپه is the subject 'the host/army'. بر هم افتاد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'fell apart / collapsed' (بر + هم + افتاد). The conjunction و links the clauses; چندی is an adverb 'for a while / after a short time'; بمرد is an older past form meaning 'he died' (the prefixed ب is an archaic/epic verbal prefix marking the past).
segments.s668-g84: همان is a demonstrative/identifying determiner 'that same'. بخت بد is a noun phrase 'ill fortune / bad luck'. کام‌کاری is the thing deprived (fulfilment of desire, success). ببرد here is a past lexical verb meaning 'took away / deprived' (classical preterite form).
segments.s668-g85: سپاه اندر آمد is 'the army came in/advanced' with اندر آمد as a compound verb 'entered/approached'. پس is a temporal connector 'then/afterwards'. پشت پیل is a prepositional/location phrase 'behind the elephant' (پشت + noun). The order places the motion first and the following locative frame after it.
segments.s668-g86: زمین شد = 'the ground became' (شد = became). بکردار functions adverbially/phrastically here as 'in the manner of / like' (classical be‑کردار ≈ 'behaving as'); دریای نیل is a genitive phrase 'the sea of the Nile' that completes the comparison. Together: 'the earth became like the Nile‑sea.'
segments.s668-g87: تلی بود is 'there was a mound' (تلی = heap/mound, بود = was). خرم is a predicative adjective 'pleasant / lush'. بدان جایگاه is a prepositional phrase 'in/at that place' formed from the prefix ب + دان (classical compound, functioning like modern بدان = 'in that / at that').
segments.s668-g88: پس is a linking adverb 'then/thereafter' (here introducing position). پشت آن رنج‌دیده سپاه is a noun phrase 'behind that racked / battle‑worn army': پشت = 'behind', آن = demonstrative 'that', رنج‌دیده is a participial adjective 'suffering / worn', modifying سپاه 'army'.
segments.s668-g153: زِ کشته: a prepositional/poetic phrase 'from/on account of the slain' placed before the clause. چو introduces a comparison/time clause 'when/as', and دریای خون شد زمین is an inverted predication: دریای خون (predicate noun) + شد (past 'became') + زمین (subject) — literally 'the earth became a sea of blood'. Poetic word order puts the verb and predicate before the subject.
segments.s668-g154: به هرگوشه‌ای: adverbial phrase 'in/at every corner' (به + distributive هرگوشه‌ای). مانده is a perfect/participial sense 'remained/was left', agreeing with the following nominal اسبی ('a horse' used substantively). به زین is a short adverbial 'in/with saddle' (به + زین) describing the horse's condition.
segments.s668-g155: همی‌گشت is an archaic/progressive past form (همی- + گشت) meaning 'was going/kept going' or 'went about'. The subject بهرام follows the verb (verb‑initial, common in classical Persian). گرد سپاه is a circumstantial phrase 'around/about the army' (گرد used adverbially/postpositionally).
segments.s668-g156: که تا introduces a subordinate/purposive clause linked to the preceding verb 'he rode about' — roughly 'in order that / to the end that'. کشته ز ایران is 'the slain from/of Iran' (ز = 'from/of'), and the second که + یابد makes a relative/purpose clause 'whom he might find on the road' — یابد is classical present/subjunctive-ish 'find' (optative/intentional sense). به راه is the locative 'on the road'.
segments.s668-g157: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that / thereafter'. بخراد برزین بگفت is an idiomatic poetic ordering: the speaker's name (بخراد) followed by the addressee (برزین) and then بگفت 'he said' — i.e. 'Bakhraad said to Barzin.' Classical Persian often omits explicit dative markers in such sequences.
segments.s668-g158: که here introduces the direct quotation or content of what was said. یک روز is the temporal expression 'one day/for a day'. با رنج ما = 'with our suffering' (با + noun + possessive ما). باش is the imperative 'be!' addressed to the addressee; جفت is used as 'companion/one who pairs with' (i.e. 'be our companion'). The whole is an imperative plea: 'Be, for one day, a companion in our suffering.'
segments.s668-g159: نگه کن is an imperative 'look!' formed by noun نگه ('gaze/watch') + imperative کن ('do'), a common classical construction. کز ایرانیان کشته is a relative phrase 'who/which (is) slain among the Iranians' (کز = 'from/among'), and کیست puts the interrogative کی 'who' before the copula ست 'is' — 'who is it?'.
segments.s668-g160: کزان 'from that (one)' links back to the preceding relative idea. درد ما را = 'our pain/sorrow' with را marking the definite object/topic. بباید + گریست is an impersonal/modal construction 'it is necessary to weep' or 'one must weep' (بباید = 'must/ought', گریست = 'to weep' / infinitival/older verbal form).
segments.s668-g273: 'رسیدند' is a simple past, 3rd-person plural verb 'they arrived'; 'یکسر' is an adverb meaning 'all at once / altogether' that qualifies the arrival; 'به‌توران زمین' is the destination expressed by the preposition-like phrase 'to Turan, the land' (توران = Turan, زمین = land/country). The verb comes first and is followed by adverbial and destination phrases in this poetic word order.
segments.s668-g274: This is a coordinated noun phrase naming the arrivals: 'سواران ترک' = 'the horsemen of the Turks' and 'دلیران چین' = 'the brave ones of Chin/China'. Adjective/national identifier follows the head noun (سواران ترک = riders + Turk). The conjunction 'و' simply links the two groups.
segments.s668-g275: 'چو آمد' is a temporal clause 'when he came'; 'بپرموده' is a past participle/participial adjective formed with the b‑ prefix meaning 'being instructed/well‑taught' and it modifies the (implicit) subject of the main clause; 'زان آگهی' = 'from/by that news' (زان = 'from that', آگهی = 'news/information'), giving the cause or source of the instruction. The whole segment makes a subordinate temporal/circumstantial clause.
segments.s668-g276: 'بینداخت' is a past, 3rd-person singular verb 'he threw/cast (off)'; the phrase 'از سر' literally 'from (the) head' indicates removal from the head; 'کلاه مهی' = 'a hat described as مهی, literally 'moon‑like' or 'moon‑beautiful' (an epithet). Word order is verb then its object phrase; adjective مهی postmodifies کلاه in typical poetic style.
segments.s668-g277: 'خروشی بر آمد' is a nominal + verb clause where 'خروشی' (a cry/roar/clamor) is the subject and 'بر آمد' (literally 'came up') functions as 'arose' — in Persian 'برآمد' commonly means 'arose/was raised'. 'ز ترکان' indicates the source/agent 'from the Turks'; 'به‌زار' is an adverbial phrase 'in distress/with lamentation' modifying the manner of the cry. Together: 'a cry of lamentation arose from the Turks.'
segments.s668-g278: 'برآن' = 'on/for that reason' or 'because of that'; 'مهتران' here names the affected people (the chiefs/leaders) — the exact lemma senses in the supplied list do not match this usage, so treat it as 'the chiefs'; 'تلخ شد روزگار' is an idiomatic predicate 'the time/fortune became bitter' (تلخ شد = 'became bitter', روزگار = 'times/fortune'), so the clause means 'for those chiefs, times/fortune turned bitter.'
segments.s668-g279: 'همه' is the universal quantifier 'all/everyone' modifying the following body‑parts; 'سر پر از گرد' = 'heads full of dust' uses the construction 'X پر از Y' ('X full of Y'), here with 'سر' (head) and 'گرد' (dust); 'دیده پر آب' parallels this with 'eyes full of water' i.e. tears. Adjectival 'پر' follows the noun in these poetic noun‑phrases.
segments.s668-g280: This short clause lists subsequent actions/states. 'کسی' is an indefinite 'someone' (not negative here); 'رانبد' is a past verb meaning 'drove away / ran off' (here used intransitively as 'ran off' or 'departed'); 'خورد' is the past of 'خوردن' ('ate') and describes a following action; 'و آرام و خواب' are nouns 'and calm and sleep' used to indicate the resulting quiet/rest. The line strings actions together without explicit subjects for each verb — a common poetic compression.
segments.s668-g345: بهره جایی که ویران بود is a noun phrase with a relative clause. بهره here functions as a noun ('a portion/part' or 'place/lot') and is followed by جایی ('a place'), which is then modified by the relative clause introduced by که. ویران is the adjective 'ruined' and بود is the past copula placed at the end of the clause ('that was ruined').
segments.s668-g346: رباطی که اندر بیابان بود is another noun (رباطی, 'a caravanserai') followed by a relative clause. که introduces the clause; اندر is a classical/prepositional locative 'in/within'; بیابان is 'desert'; بود is the past copula 'was', final in the clause.
segments.s668-g347: کند is the main verb 'makes/does' (third-person) placed before its complements for emphasis. یکسر آباد is an adverbial/adjectival phrase meaning 'entirely/prosperous' (یکسر = entirely, آباد = prosperous/inhabited). جوینده modifies مرد (the seeker-man = 'the man who seeks' or 'the seeker'), with the modifier preceding the noun.
segments.s668-g348: نباشد is a classical negative copular form used impersonally to express non-existence ('there is not' / 'is not'); it negates the following locative phrase. به here functions as a locative/directional preposition ('on/in/along'); راه means 'road/path'; اندرون is 'inside/inner (quarters)' and together به راه اندرون = 'on/along the inner road/way'. بیم و درد is a coordinated noun phrase 'fear and sorrow/affliction'.
segments.s668-g349: ببخشید is used in the sense of 'remit/forgive/waive' (here: remit or give up payment) and is the past or narrational form. پس is a connective 'then/afterwards'. چار ساله forms a temporal/adjectival phrase 'four‑year' (چهارساله = 'for four years' or 'of four years'), and خراج is the tax/tribute that is being remitted.
segments.s668-g350: به درویش is a dative phrase 'to the dervish' with به marking the beneficiary. و is the coordinating 'and'. آن را که is a classical sequence where آن ('that') plus the object marker را introduces a relative clause (آن را که = 'that which' / 'what'). بد modifies تخت عاج ('the (ill/ill‑favored) ivory throne'); تخت عاج ('throne of ivory') is a noun phrase with عاج ('ivory') qualifying تخت ('throne').
segments.s668-g351: نبشتند is past tense, third-person plural 'they wrote/inscribed'. پس is the temporal/consecutive 'then'. نامه is the direct object 'a letter'. از شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'from the king', indicating the author/source of the letter.
segments.s668-g352: به هرکشوری is a prepositional phrase 'to every country/land' (به marking the recipient/target). سوی is another directional preposition 'toward/unto' used here to introduce the next object. هرنامدار is a compound meaning 'every famed/notable (person)'—the intended recipients in each land.
segments.s668-g313: Word order places the recipient before the verb: شهنشاه را uses را to mark the (definite) recipient 'the shah'. زان سخن ('from that speech') modifies مژده ('good news'), and داد is a simple past 'gave'. Together: 'He gave the shah the good news from that speech.'
segments.s668-g314: که introduces the reported clause. جاوید بادا is an optative formula: جاوید 'eternal' + بادا 'may it be' (archaic/poetic optative). جهان‌دار is the subject/title 'world‑ruler' and شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy' — so the clause reads as a wish: 'May the world‑ruler be ever (جاوید) happy.'
segments.s668-g315: که links this clause to the preceding. بهرام is the subject; بر + place (ساوه) means 'against/over Saveh'. پیروز is an adjectival predicate 'victorious' and گشت is the past tense 'became' (i.e. 'became victorious').
segments.s668-g316: به رزم here expresses manner/instrument ('by/in battle'). اندرون + گیتی together modify افروز (a descriptive epithet 'world‑illuminating'); گشت again is 'became'. So the clause says he 'became (the) giti‑afroz' — i.e. the one who illumines the world — through battle. This is a poetic nominal predicate formed with افروز + گشت.
segments.s668-g317: سبک مرد is an epithet placed before the name ('the light/quick man' = 'nimble warrior'). بهرام را uses را to mark the person being summoned; پیش خواند is a compound meaning 'called/summoned forward' (خواند here 'call/summon' in past). The phrase therefore: 'He called Bahram — the nimble warrior — forward.'
segments.s668-g318: وزان = و ز آن ('and from those/of them'); نامدارانش = 'his renowned men' (suffix -ش = 'his'). برتر here describes the one placed 'above/foremost', and نشاند is the past transitive 'caused to sit' (archaic: 'set/placed (seated)'). The clause means 'and from those famous men he set the foremost (in a higher place).'
segments.s668-g319: فرستاده (the messenger) is the subject and گفت is the reporting verb ('said') introducing direct speech. ای is the vocative 'O', and سر افراز شاه is a compound epithet 'head‑raised / exalted king' (literally 'head‑raising king') used as the addressee. In context this is the customary vocative formula.
segments.s668-g320: به کام تو is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to/for your desire'; شد is past 'became' and forms with the following nominal phrase کام آن رزم‌گاه ('the desire/wish of that battlefield') — a poetic inversion: literally 'to your desire became the desire of that battlefield', i.e. 'the battlefield's wish has been fulfilled for you.' The repetition of کام is a stylistic/poetic device.
segments.s668-g305: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival form meaning 'seated' used here as a circumstantial participle describing the subject; جهان‌دار is the subject noun 'world‑ruler' (the king); با موبدان is a prepositional phrase 'with the mobeds/priests' indicating with whom he is seated. Word order places the participle before the noun for a descriptive effect.
segments.s668-g306: همی‌گفت uses the archaic iterative/perfective prefix همی + گفت to mean 'kept saying' or 'was saying' (continuous past); کای is a vocative exclamation 'O'; نامور is an epithet 'renowned'; بخردان is a vocative/plural adjective 'wise (ones)' addressing the priests. The segment introduces direct speech directed at the mobeds.
segments.s668-g307: دو هفته is a temporal phrase 'two weeks'; بدین is a contraction of به + این (here written as the two tokens ب + دین) functioning as a locative/prepositional phrase 'to/at this'; بارگاه is 'the royal court/palace'; مهی is an adjective 'moon‑like, radiant' describing the court. The whole segment gives the time span and place where the following absence is noted.
segments.s668-g308: نیامد is simple past negative 'did not come'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; بهرام is the proper name 'Bahram'; هیچ is the negative quantifier 'no/none'; آگهی means 'information/news'. Together: 'no news came from Bahram.'
segments.s668-g309: چه گویید is an interrogative phrase 'what do you say' (گویید = 2nd plural present/subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'you say' in classical usage); ازین پس = از + این + پس 'from this (time) on' = 'from now on'; چه شاید بدن places چه (again, emphatic interrogative/exclamative) before شاید 'perhaps' and بدن 'the body' as the topical object that the following verb will concern. Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis: the possible affected object (بدن) precedes the verb phrase in the next segment.
segments.s668-g310: بباید is the impersonal/modal construction 'it is necessary/one must' (here conveying 'must'); بدین is intended as به + این ('to/with these') but in the tokenization appears as ب + دین (difficult to match exact lemma sense), marking direction/association with داستان‌ها; داستان‌ها is the plural 'these tales/stories'; زدن is the infinitive 'to strike/hit' or more generally 'to strike/punish' and functions as the complement of بباید. Together with the previous segment the sense is 'perhaps a body must be struck by these tales' (i.e., the tales will have violent effect/punishment on a body).
segments.s668-g311: همانگه is an adverb 'at that very moment'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that'; گفت here is the past tense 'said' (note: the listed lemma senses are dialogic rather than the simple 'say', so the best reading is 'he said'); این سخن is 'these words' and شهریار is 'the king' (subject) — so 'at that moment when the king said these words.'
segments.s668-g312: بیامد is simple past 'came'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; درگاه is 'the royal court/presence' (place of reception); سالار is 'the commander/chief' and بار is a noun whose exact nuance depends on context (here part of a compound referring to the commander’s court/presence). Together: 'a (messenger) came from the court/presence of the commander.'
segments.s668-g417: همی‌گفت is a past habitual/progressive narrative form meaning “kept saying.” The clause کین از هنرها یکیست uses the pattern X از Y یکی‌ست = “X is one of Y,” where از introduces the set (هنرها) and یکیست is a timeless copula (‘is one’).
segments.s668-g418: اگر چه here functions as a concessive ‘although’ (classical compound of اگر + چه). سپه‌شان is a possessive compound ‘their host/army’ and کنون is a temporal adverb ‘now’; اندکیست is an adjectival/categorical copula meaning ‘is small/there is little.’
segments.s668-g419: سواران و گردان is a coordinate noun phrase ‘horsemen and ranks/troops.’ پر مایه‌اند is an adjectival phrase: پر+مایه = ‘rich/abundant in resource,’ with the plural copula -اند marking them as such.
segments.s668-g420: ز here means ‘from/among’ (poetic literary use). گردن‌کشان is a plural agentive noun ‘neck-cutters’ i.e. rebels/insolents; برترین is a superlative ‘foremost,’ and پایه‌اند (پایه + اند) indicates they ‘constitute the base’ or ‘are the principal support.’
segments.s668-g421: سلیحست is an archaic/poetic copula meaning ‘is armed/well-equipped.’ وبهرام‌شان is a linked phrase ‘and their Bahram’ (و + proper name + possessive suffix), and پیش‌رو here means ‘the one who goes ahead / the vanguard/leader.’
segments.s668-g422: که introduces a result/clause (‘so that’); گردد is a subjunctive/optative of گشتن used here as ‘becomes’/‘turns into.’ سنان (spears) is the subject, پیش او = ‘before him,’ and خار و خو are the predicate nouns joined by و (literal: ‘thorn and habit’); the line uses a compact, poetic image.
segments.s668-g423: به پیروزی ساوه is an instrumental/causal prepositional phrase ‘by/through the victory at Saveh’ (به used in the sense ‘by’), شاه is the agent ‘the king,’ and اندرون here is the object/place ‘the interior/within’ (he took/entered the inner part).
segments.s668-g424: گرفته دل is a compact phrase where گرفته (seized/overcome) applies to دل ‘heart’ = ‘his heart was seized/taken.’ مست گشته is a compound verbal phrase ‘became intoxicated,’ and به خون (instrumental) means ‘with blood / by bloodshed’ (i.e. he revels in blood).
segments.s668-g361: Numeric determiner + noun order: یکی (a/one) precedes تخت سیمین (a compound noun: تخت 'throne' + adjective سیمین 'silvery/silver') and the verb فرستاد is the 3rd-person past 'he sent'. نیز is a postpositive adverb 'also', attaching to the whole clause to mark this gift as added to others.
segments.s668-g362: Standard noun phrase list: the numeral دو (implicit here as 'دو نعلین') precedes the noun نعلین (sandals), followed by the adjective زرین (golden) placed after the noun; و links to the following quantifying phrase هر گونه چیز ('every/any kind of thing'), where هر modifies گونه and together they govern چیز ('thing').
segments.s668-g363: A prepositional range: ز (from) + place name هیتال (Hital), تا (as far as) introduces the end point, followed by a paired element پیش رود برک treated here as a toponymic sequence (preshift/river name); the whole phrase marks the geographic extent 'from Hital up to the Barak (stream)'.
segments.s668-g364: Structure: indirect object marked by به + name (به بهرام 'to/for Bahram') followed by بخشید, 3rd-person past 'he bestowed/gave'. و links a following clause; بنوشت is a past-form verb 'he wrote' (archaic/polite past formation) taking چک as its object (here 'a note' or short message).
segments.s668-g365: بفرمود is a courtly/polite past-tense verb 'he commanded/said'. کان here is an archaic demonstrative/particle introducing the object clause, and خواسته (a noun or past participle) names 'the requested/desired thing'; بر سپاه places the beneficiary with بر used in the older sense 'toward/to' + سپاه 'army', yielding 'to the army'.
segments.s668-g366: Imperative/optative instruction: ببخش is a command 'give/grant' (here 'hand over'), followed by the relative pronoun-like آنچ 'that which/whatever' and آوردی, 2nd-person past 'you brought' (used to refer to what was brought). از رزم‌گاه is a simple prepositional complement 'from the battlefield'.
segments.s668-g367: مگرگنج is a compound: مگر (except/save) + گنج (treasure), i.e., 'except the treasure'. ویژه is an attributive adjective 'special/reserved', تن here means 'person' or 'personally' (an idiom meaning 'belonging to the person of'), and ساوه شاه is a genitive-style sequence 'the king of Saveh' (place name + title).
segments.s668-g368: Relative clause headed by که 'which/that'. آورد is the verb 'to bring' (here part of the passive/modal construction), with باید 'it must/ought' following it — older Persian often places باید after the verb in such clauses; ب + دین together form بدین 'to this', and بارگاه is the destination 'court/palace' — overall: 'which must be brought to this court.'
segments.s668-g393: This is a prepositional/location phrase. بدو (به + او) here means 'to/at that' (pointing to a place), followed by منزل بلخ 'the dwelling/place of Balkh'. هر دو سپاه means 'both armies' (هر + دو = 'both' in combination). Word order is typical Persian: location + topic (the two armies).
segments.s668-g394: گزیدند is a past‑tense plural verb 'they chose/selected'. شایسته is an adjective meaning 'appropriate/suitable' modifying the following noun rasm-gah. دو رزم‌گاه is a numeral + noun 'two battle-places' (the phrase implies each side has its ground or that two grounds were picked). The verb governs the whole preceding noun phrase.
segments.s668-g395: میان is the preposition 'between' and introduces the two nouns دو لشکر. دو فرسنگ بود is an existential statement with بود (past 'there was/was') stating the distance: 'two farsangs separated them.' The verb is third person singular past, agreeing with the distance phrase.
segments.s668-g396: که here introduces an explanatory or relative clause ('which/that'): 'that the width (پهنای) of the plain (دشت) from/of the battle‑gate/line (از در جنگ) was (بود)'. پهنای دشت is a genitive construct 'the breadth of the plain'; از در جنگ is an idiomatic locative 'from the battle‑gate/line'. بود is the copula in past tense.
segments.s668-g397: دگر روز is an adverbial time expression 'another/next day'. بهرام is the subject. جنگی here functions as an object/adjunct meaning 'to battle' or 'for combat'. برفت is an archaic past form meaning 'he went forth/rode forth' (a past tense verb); the provided senses for this token do not match this usage, so the sense index is not applicable.
segments.s668-g398: به دیدار is 'to the meeting/visit of' (به + noun). گردان پرموده تفت is a noun phrase: گردان 'troop' modified by پرموده 'well‑trained/experienced' and then تفت (here likely a proper name or epithet attached to the troop). Grammatically this whole phrase is the goal of برفت ('he went to visit the experienced troop of/called Tft').
segments.s668-g399: نگه کرد is a compound verb made of the noun نگه 'gaze/watch' + کرد 'did' and means 'he looked/kept watch'. پرموده را is the definite object marked by را ('the experienced one'), and بدید is the past of 'to see' — the clause consists of two coordinated actions: he looked and he saw that experienced man.
segments.s668-g400: ز (poetic variant of از) + هامون 'plain' gives the origin 'from the plain'. یکی is an indefinite 'one / someone' acting as subject. تند بالا is an adverbial cluster 'swiftly upward' (تند = swift, بالا = up). گزید is a past verb here meaning 'sprang/leapt up' in context; none of the provided dictionary senses match this poetic/archaic usage, so its sense index is -1.
segments.s668-g97: نشست + از + بر form a classical expression meaning 'to alight from (the back of)'. The verb نشَست (past) here means 'dismounted/alighted'. از ... بر together mark 'off/from upon' the mount (تازی اسب سمند = 'the Arabian war-horse'). Word order in poetry places the prepositional phrase after the verb.
segments.s668-g98: همی‌تاخت is the imperfective/continuous verbal form (همی- prefix + تاخت) meaning 'was charging' or 'rushed on'. ترسان is a participial/adjectival word 'frightened/afraid' describing the subject (the steed). ز is the poetic form of از 'from/because of', and بیم گزند gives the cause 'for fear of harm/injury'. The adjective/phrase follows the verb, as is common in poetry.
segments.s668-g99: بر ساوه is a locative phrase 'at/to Saveh' (بر used for place), followed by the subject بهرام. چون پیل مست is a simile 'like an elephant, intoxicated/drunken' (چون + noun + adjective). The adjective مست is used figuratively; poetry often places the simile immediately after the subject.
segments.s668-g100: This is a pair of instrumental/attendant phrases describing Bahram: کمندی به بازو = 'a lasso on (his) arm' (به introducing location on the arm), and کمانی بدست = 'a bow in (his) hand' (کمانی 'bow(-related)', بدست literally 'in-hand'). In poetry the possessive pronoun is omitted; these phrases attach to the subject.
segments.s668-g101: به لشکر = 'to the army' (prepositional phrase). چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus', گفت past of 'say' introducing speech). کای is the vocative particle 'O', used to address (here) سرکشان 'the proud/rebellious ones' (vocative). Word order is 'to the army thus said O X' — typical in epic diction.
segments.s668-g102: زبخت ... بد آمد is an idiom: زبخت (literally 'by fate') + بد آمد ('badly came') = 'ill-fortune befell'. بر ایشان marks the affected party ('upon them'). نشان here is 'a sign/omen' — the whole phrase says that an omen or ill-fate has come upon them. The order places the idiomatic verb phrase before the direct phrase 'بر ایشان نشان'.
segments.s668-g103: نه هنگام رازست = 'it is not the time for secrecy' (نه negates, هنگام 'time', رازست 'is a secret / secrecy' used predicatively). و روز سخن = 'and (this is) the day/time for speech' (روز + سخن, 'day of speaking'). The clause contrasts secrecy with open speech/action.
segments.s668-g104: بتازید is a hortative/imperative-like present form meaning 'rush/attack!' (ب- prefix + تازید). با introduces the instrument 'with', تیغ‌های کهن = 'your old/ancient swords' (تیغ‌های plural + adjective کهن). The phrase is a command to charge using old blades; in poetry the imperative sense is common in this verb form.
segments.s668-g401: سپه را (object + object marker) comes before the verb in typical Persian word order; here 'را' marks the definite object 'the host'. 'سراسر همه' is an adverbial/emphatic phrase meaning 'all through / entirely' placed between object and verb for emphasis. برنشاند is a past tense/ perfective poetic form meaning 'set out / deployed' (third person singular).
segments.s668-g402: چنان شد که is a result clause: چنان شد 'it became so' introduces the consequence marked by که 'that'. در دشت is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the plain'. جایی نماند uses جایی 'any/place' plus the past negative نماند 'was not left / remained not' to express 'no place remained.'
segments.s668-g403: This clause follows Subject–Verb–Object order: سپه (subject) دید (past 'saw') پرموده (object, 'the experienced/veteran one'). چندانک 'to such an extent' is an adverb of degree that here prepares the following result clause; دشت (the plain) is positioned to become the subject of that following result verb.
segments.s668-g404: ز دیدار ایشان is an ablative/causal phrase 'from (the) sight/appearance of them' introduced by poetic ز. همی precedes the verb here as an archaic/progressive/emphatic particle; خیره گشت is a compound meaning 'became astonished / was transfixed' (گشت = became). The clause therefore states the plain's reaction to seeing them.
segments.s668-g405: The conjunction و links this clause to the previous action. را appears here as the definite object marker attached to an omitted noun/pronoun ('him') — anaphoric omission common in classical Persian. در پیش آن لشکرش is a locative phrase 'in the presence of / before that army of his' (پیش = before/in the presence of; لشکرش = his army). دید is simple past 'saw.'
segments.s668-g406: This is a participial/result phrase describing posture or motion: به گردون (toward the sky/heavens) + برآورده (a perfective/participial form 'raised / brought up') + جنگی سرش (جنگی qualifying سر 'his head' — 'his warlike head'). The possessive suffix ش attaches to سر. In classical usage such participial phrases follow the noun they modify and describe a simultaneous or prior action.
segments.s668-g407: غمی گشت is a light nominal construction: غمی 'sorrow' + گشت 'became' = 'he became sorrowful.' با لشکر خویش گفت 'said to/with his host' — با + لشکر خویش ('his army') indicates the addressee; گفت here is the past tense 'he said' (the lemma list gives 'گفت' as noun senses, but context is the verb 'گفتن' in past).
segments.s668-g408: که introduces reported speech/content of what he said. این پیش‌رو را is the definite object 'this vanguard/forerunner' (پیش‌رو = forerunner/leader). هزبرست جفت is an obscure classical phrase: هزبرست is a rare/opaque adjective and جفت here functions as 'match/pair' — together it conveys 'a match worth a thousand' or similar hyperbolic appraisal; the exact lexical meaning is uncertain in modern Persian but the construction is adjectival complement to پیش‌رو.
segments.s668-g353: 'که' here is the relative/conjunction 'that/when' introducing the clause. 'بهرام' is the proper name (subject). 'پیروز شد' is a past tense/perfect-like expression using 'شد' as a copular/auxiliary meaning 'became/was victorious.' 'بر سپاه' uses the preposition 'بر' = 'over/against' with 'سپاه' (army) as the object; the order is subject + predicate + prepositional object in poetic diction.
segments.s668-g354: The verb 'بریدند' is third-person plural past 'they cut/they severed.' In classical/poetic word order the adjective 'بی‌بر' (here used as an epithet 'ill‑fated' modifying the king) precedes the noun phrase. 'سر ساوه شاه' is an inverted genitive: literal order is 'سرِ شاهِ ساوه' ('the head of the king of Saveh'), with the possessor after the governed noun — a common poetic inversion. So the clause says 'they cut off the head of the ill‑fated king of Saveh.'
segments.s668-g355: 'پرستنده بد شاه' is a noun phrase in which 'پرستنده' (worshipper/devotee) is qualified by 'بد' (bad); 'شاه' follows to indicate possession or association ('the king's bad worshipper' or 'the king, a bad worshipper' depending on context). 'در هفت روز' is a temporal phrase 'within seven days' or 'in seven days' (در + numeral + روز).
segments.s668-g356: 'به هشتم' is a temporal phrase 'on the eighth [day]' with 'به' marking the temporal point. 'چو' is a subordinating conjunction 'when.' 'بفروخت' is a past-tense verb 'he sold / he parted with' (classical past form). 'گیتی فروز' is a compound/name meaning 'world‑illuminator' (Giti‑Fruz) and functions here as the object of 'بفروخت.' The whole clause reads 'on the eighth, when he sold Giti‑Fruz.'
segments.s668-g357: فرستاده پهلوان رابخواند is parsed as فرستاده (the messenger) + پهلوان (of the champion) + را + بخواند (here fused as رابخواند): the verb means “he summoned/called.” The particle را is attached to the object and the verb is past 3rd‑person.
segments.s668-g358: به مهر is an adverbial phrase “with kindness/affection.” از بر نامداران is an older locative/phrase meaning “among/in the presence of the renowned” (از = from/among; بر gives the sense 'before/in front of'); نشاند is an archaic past meaning “(caused to) sit” = “seated him.”
segments.s668-g359: مر is an old dative/indirect‑object pronoun “to me.” آن نامه را is the definite direct object “that letter” (را marks the object). خوب پاسخ is the noun phrase “a good reply,” and نبشت is an archaic past tense “wrote” (3rd‑person). Word order places the indirect object before the object and verb.
segments.s668-g360: درختی به باغ بزرگی بکشت = he planted a tree in/into a large garden. درختی = “a tree;” به + باغ gives the goal/location “into/at the garden”; بزرگی is an adjectival noun meaning “large” or “great,” and بکشت is an archaic past verb here meaning “he planted/caused to be planted.”
segments.s668-g49: همه دل به خون ریختن is a fronted noun phrase built from دل + the verbal-noun phrase به خون ریختن (“shedding/spilling blood”), meaning “every heart (to) bleed”; برنهید is a 2nd-person-plural jussive/imperative-like verb ('put/bring about' or 'arm'), so the whole segment is an order: “make every heart shed blood / make all hearts bleed.” The object phrase appears before the verb (typical poetic inversion).
segments.s668-g50: سپر بر سر آرید و خنجر دهید is a sequence of imperatives. سپر بر سر آرید literally 'bring/put shield on (your) head' (سپر + postpositional بر سر + آرید imperative 'bring/put'); the coordinating و links the second command خنجر دهید ('give/brandish daggers' — دهید here is an imperative 'give/offer/brandish'). Word order is object/place phrase before the verb, a normal poetic ordering.
segments.s668-g51: ز یزدان precedes the clause and gives the reference 'with respect to God / of God'; نباشد کسی ناامید uses نباشد in a jussive/negative-subjunctive sense 'let there not be' or 'should not be', followed by کسی ناامید 'anyone hopeless' — together: 'Let no one be hopeless of God' (i.e. do not despair of God).
segments.s668-g52: وگر is the conditional 'and if / but if'; تیره بینند روز سپید reads as 'they see the day dark' (تیره modifies the way the day is seen), with بینند a 3rd-plural present/subjunctive 'see' and روز سپید 'a bright/white day' as the contrasting image — the sense is: 'and if they see the day dark, (yet) it will be bright.' The verb is general/gnomic rather than tense-specific.
segments.s668-g53: چنین 'thus' modifies the speech verb گفت ('said'); با مهتران is the prepositional phrase 'to/with the chiefs' (the addressees), and ساوه شاه is the agent 'the king of Saveh' (title following the verb in poetic word order). The whole is a reporting clause: 'Thus said the king of Saveh to the chiefs.'
segments.s668-g54: که introduces reported content ('that / namely / so that'); پیلان بیارید is an imperative clause 'bring the elephants' (پیلان 'elephants', بیارید imperative 'bring' or 'cause to come'); پیش سپاه 'before the army' or 'in front of the host' gives the place/position. The clause is the command reported by the previous segment.
segments.s668-g55: به انبوه لشکر به جنگ آورید is an imperative sentence: به انبوه لشکر 'the vast host/army' (انبوه modifying لشکر) and به جنگ آورید 'bring/lead to battle' (آورید imperative 'bring/cause to come to'). The repeated preposition به marks direction/purpose ('to battle').
segments.s668-g56: بدیشان جهان تار و تنگ آورید is another imperative directed 'to them' (بدیشان = 'onto/against them'); جهان is the object 'the world/life' and تار و تنگ are coordinated adjectives describing how to make it for them ('dark/bleak and narrow/tight'); آورید again is the verb 'bring/make'. The whole means 'make the world dark and cramped for them.'
